blocks.heading: A computer system has 3 main parts. 💻
blocks.html:
Hardware, software, and the user.

All 3 parts work together.

blocks.heading: Hardware is the parts you can touch. 🖱️
blocks.html:
A mouse, a keyboard, and a screen are hardware.

If you can pick it up, it is hardware.

blocks.heading: Software is the programs inside the computer. 🧩
blocks.html:
You cannot touch software.

Software gives the hardware instructions to follow.

blocks.heading: The user is the person using the computer. 🙋
blocks.html:
That could be you!

The user tells the computer what to do.

blocks.heading: A computer system helps the user do a task. 🎯
blocks.html:
It might help you write, draw, or play a game.

The system is built around what the user needs.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: The parts you can touch 🖱️
The programs inside the computer 🧩
The person using the computer 🙋
Hardware, software and a user working together 💻

question.text: Which of these is hardware? 🖱️
question.explanation: Hardware is any part you can touch. A mouse is hardware.

question.text: What is software? 🧩
question.explanation: Software is a set of programs. You cannot touch software.

question.text: Who is the user? 🙋
question.explanation: The user is the person who uses the computer system.

blocks.heading: There are 2 main types of computer systems. 💻
blocks.html:
Desktop computers stay in one place.

Mobile devices can move with you. 📱

blocks.heading: A desktop computer sits on a desk. 🖥️
blocks.html:
It has a separate screen, keyboard, and mouse.

It needs power from a wall plug.

blocks.heading: A mobile device is small and light. 📱
blocks.html:
Laptops, tablets, and phones are mobile devices.

You can carry them and use them anywhere.

blocks.heading: Input devices send information into the computer. ⌨️
blocks.html:
A keyboard, mouse, and microphone are input devices.

Input means "going in".

blocks.heading: Output devices show information to the user. 🖨️
blocks.html:
A screen, printer, and speaker are output devices.

Output means "coming out".

blocks.heading: The processor is the brain of the computer. 🧠
blocks.html:
It is also called the CPU.

Processing means working out the answer.

blocks.heading: Storage devices save your files. 💾
blocks.html:
A hard drive and a USB stick are storage devices.

Your files stay saved even when the power is off.

blocks.heading: Communication devices connect computers together. 📡
blocks.html:
A modem and a network card are examples.

They let computers send and receive data.

blocks.title: Match each hardware type to its job. 🧩

pairs.definition: Sends information into the computer ⌨️
Shows information to the user 🖨️
Works out the answer (the CPU) 🧠
Saves your files 💾
Connects computers together 📡

question.text: Which is a mobile device? 📱
question.explanation: A laptop is small and light. You can carry it with you, so it is a mobile device.

question.text: Which device is an input device? ⌨️
question.explanation: A microphone sends sound into the computer. Input means information going in.

question.text: What does the CPU do? 🧠
question.explanation: The CPU is the brain of the computer. It processes information.

blocks.heading: There are 3 main types of software. 🧩
blocks.html:
Operating system, application, and utility.

Each type does a different job.

blocks.heading: The operating system runs the whole computer. ⚙️
blocks.html:
Windows and macOS are operating systems.

It starts up first and controls everything else.

blocks.heading: An application helps you do a task. 📝
blocks.html:
Word, a game, and a web browser are applications.

You open an application to do something specific.

blocks.heading: A utility looks after the computer. 🛠️
blocks.html:
Antivirus software is a utility.

Utilities check, clean, and protect the system.

blocks.heading: Troubleshooting means fixing a problem. 🔧
blocks.html:
Computers sometimes stop working properly.

Troubleshooting finds the cause and fixes it.

blocks.heading: Try these steps when something goes wrong. 🔁
blocks.html:
1. Restart the computer.
2. Check all the cables.
3. Close the program and open it again.
4. Ask for help if it still does not work.

blocks.title: Put these troubleshooting steps in the correct order. 🔧
Complete this sentence: The ___ is the software that runs the whole computer.
blocks.explanation: The operating system starts up first and controls everything else, like Windows or macOS.

question.text: What type of software is Windows? ⚙️
question.explanation: Windows is an operating system. It runs the whole computer.

question.text: What does antivirus software do? 🛠️
question.explanation: Antivirus is a utility. Utilities look after and protect the computer.

question.text: What should you try first if a program freezes? 🔧
question.explanation: Restarting the computer is a simple first step. It often fixes small problems.

blocks.heading: WSH means workplace safety and health. 🦺
blocks.html:
It keeps you safe when you use a computer.

Good habits stop you from getting hurt.

blocks.heading: Good lighting helps you see clearly. 💡
blocks.html:
A room that is too dark or too bright hurts your eyes.

Use soft, even light around your screen.

blocks.heading: Ventilation means fresh air in the room. 🌬️
blocks.html:
Computers can make a room feel hot and stuffy.

Fresh air helps you stay alert and comfortable.

blocks.heading: Sit with correct posture. 🪑
blocks.html:
Keep your back straight and feet flat on the floor.

Your screen should be at eye level.

blocks.heading: Take regular breaks and exercise. 🚶
blocks.html:
Stand up and stretch every 30 minutes.

Moving your body stops your muscles getting stiff.

blocks.heading: Using a computer for too long can hurt your health. ⚠️
blocks.html:
OOS — sore muscles and joints from repeating the same movement.
Back strain — pain from sitting badly.
Eye strain — sore, tired eyes from screen time.

blocks.title: Match each health risk to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Sore muscles and joints from repeating movements 🤲
Pain in your back from sitting badly 🪑
Sore, tired eyes from too much screen time 👀

question.text: What does WSH stand for? 🦺
question.explanation: WSH stands for workplace safety and health. It keeps you safe while you work.

question.text: How often should you stand up and stretch? 🚶
question.explanation: Standing and stretching every 30 minutes helps stop your muscles from getting stiff.

question.text: What causes eye strain? 👀
question.explanation: Looking at a screen for too long can make your eyes sore and tired. This is eye strain.

blocks.heading: Word processing software is used to make documents. 📝
blocks.html:
You can write letters, reports, and stories.

Examples are Microsoft Word and Google Docs.

blocks.heading: A font is the style of your letters. 🔤
blocks.html:
Different fonts look different.

Example: Arial looks plain. This looks fancy.

blocks.heading: Font size changes how big the letters are. 🔍
blocks.html:
Small text is good for long paragraphs.

Big text is good for titles and headings.

blocks.heading: Font style adds a look to your letters. 🖊️
blocks.html:
Bold makes text thick and dark.
Italic makes text slanted.
Underline puts a line under the text.

blocks.heading: Alignment is where your text sits on the page. ↔️
blocks.html: Text can be lined up left, centre, right, or justified (even on both sides).

blocks.heading: Line spacing is the gap between lines of text. 📏
blocks.html:
More space makes a document easier to read.

Less space fits more text on one page.

blocks.heading: You can add tables, pictures, and objects. 🖼️
blocks.html:
A table holds information in rows and columns.

Pictures and objects make your document more interesting.

blocks.heading: A header is text at the top of every page. ⬆️
blocks.html:
A footer is text at the bottom of every page. ⬇️

Headers and footers often show a page number or title.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: The style of your letters 🔤
Where text sits on the page ↔️
Text at the top of every page ⬆️
Text at the bottom of every page ⬇️

question.text: What does font size change? 🔍
question.explanation: Font size changes how big or small your letters look on the page.

question.text: Where is a footer found? ⬇️
question.explanation: A footer is text that repeats at the bottom of every page, like a page number.

question.text: Which alignment makes text even on both sides? ↔️
question.explanation: Justified alignment spreads text evenly so both the left and right edges line up.

blocks.heading: Formatting is changing how a document looks. 🎨
blocks.html:
Good formatting makes a document easy to read.

It does not change the words, just the look.

blocks.heading: Use bold for titles and important words. 💪
blocks.html:
Bold text stands out from normal text.

Don't bold too much, or nothing stands out.

blocks.heading: A table organises information in a grid. ▦
blocks.html:
It has rows (across) and columns (up and down).

Tables are good for lists, prices, and schedules.

blocks.heading: Tight line spacing fits more on a page. 📐
blocks.html:
Wide line spacing is easier on the eyes.

Reports often use 1.5 or double line spacing.

blocks.heading: Pictures should match your topic. 🖼️
blocks.html:
Resize a picture by dragging its corner.

Keep pictures small enough that text still fits.

blocks.heading: Page numbers go in the header or footer. 🔢
blocks.html:
They help readers find a page quickly.

Most documents number every page automatically.

blocks.heading: Keep formatting consistent. ✅
blocks.html:
Use the same font and size all through your document.

This makes your work look neat and professional.

blocks.title: Put these document formatting steps in the correct order. 📝

question.text: What is a table used for? ▦
options: Organising information in rows and columns
question.explanation: A table organises information into rows and columns, like a list or schedule.

question.text: Why should your formatting stay consistent? ✅
options: It makes the document look neat
question.explanation: Using the same font and style throughout makes your document look neat and professional.

question.text: Where can a page number be added? 🔢
question.explanation: Page numbers are usually placed in the header or footer so they appear on every page.

blocks.heading: Proofreading means checking your work for mistakes. 🔍
blocks.html:
Always proofread before you finish a document.

Mistakes are easier to fix early.

blocks.heading: Spell check finds spelling mistakes. 🔡
blocks.html:
It often shows a red wavy line under wrong words.

Right-click the word to see suggestions.

blocks.heading: Grammar check finds sentence mistakes. ✍️
blocks.html:
It often shows a blue or green wavy line.

It checks things like missing words or wrong tense.

blocks.heading: Spell check does not catch every mistake. ⚠️
blocks.html:
It cannot tell if you used the wrong word correctly spelled.

Example: "their" instead of "there". Always read your work too!

blocks.heading: Print preview shows how your document will look on paper. 🖨️
blocks.html:
Use it before you print to check the layout.

This saves paper and ink.

blocks.heading: Print options let you change how a document prints. ⚙️
blocks.html:
You can choose the number of copies.

You can also choose which pages to print, and colour or black & white.

blocks.heading: Always check before you click print. ✅
blocks.html:
Check spelling, grammar, and the print preview.

This stops you from wasting paper on mistakes.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Finds spelling mistakes 🔡
Finds sentence mistakes ✍️
Shows how the page will look on paper 🖨️
Choose copies, pages, and colour ⚙️

question.text: What does spell check find? 🔡
question.explanation: Spell check looks for words that are spelled wrong, often shown with a red wavy line.

question.text: Why should you still read your own work after spell check? ⚠️
options: Spell check cannot catch every mistake, like the wrong word used correctly spelled
question.explanation: Spell check cannot tell if a correctly spelled word is the wrong word, like "their" instead of "there".

question.text: What does print preview show you? 🖨️
options: How the document will look on paper
question.explanation: Print preview shows exactly how your document will look once it is printed on paper.

blocks.heading: A file is one saved item on your computer. 📄
blocks.html:
A document, photo, or song are all files.

Every file has a name.

blocks.heading: A folder holds files together. 📁
blocks.html:
Folders help you stay organised.

You can put folders inside other folders too.

blocks.heading: Give your files clear, simple names. 🏷️
blocks.html:
A good name tells you what is inside.

Example: English_Essay_Draft1 is better than Document1.

blocks.heading: Naming conventions are rules for naming files. 📋
blocks.html:
Many schools and workplaces use one style.

Example: Subject_Task_Date, like Maths_Test_2026-06-29.

blocks.heading: Version control means tracking changes to a file. 🔄
blocks.html:
Add a version number when you save a new draft.

Example: Essay_v1, Essay_v2, Essay_v3.

blocks.heading: Version control stops you losing your best work. 💾
blocks.html:
If a new version breaks, you can go back to an older one.

Never overwrite your only saved copy without a backup.

blocks.heading: A password protects your files and accounts. 🔒
blocks.html:
Use a password that is hard for others to guess.

Never share your password with people you don't trust.

blocks.heading: A strong password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols. 🔑
blocks.html:
Example: Sunset7!Kite is strong.

Example: 123456 is weak and easy to guess.

blocks.title: Complete this strong file name: Maths_Homework_______
blocks.explanation: Adding a version number like v1 helps you track changes if you save more drafts later, such as v2 or v3.

question.text: What does a folder do? 📁
question.explanation: A folder holds files together so you can stay organised and find them easily.

question.text: Why use version control, like naming files v1, v2, v3? 🔄
options: It tracks changes so you can go back to an older version
question.explanation: Version control tracks changes over time. If something goes wrong, you can return to an older saved version.

question.text: Which is the strongest password? 🔑
question.explanation: A strong password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols. This makes it much harder to guess.

blocks.heading: Presentation software helps you make slides. 💻
blocks.html:
Slides are like digital pages you show on a screen.

You use words, pictures and sound to share ideas.

blocks.heading: The layout is how things are placed on a slide. 📐
blocks.html:
A title might go at the top.

A picture might go in the middle.

Good layout makes a slide easy to read.

blocks.heading: A template is a ready-made design. 🎨
blocks.html:
It already has colours, fonts and a layout.

You just add your own words and pictures.

blocks.heading: A transition is how one slide changes to the next. 🔄
blocks.html:
Example: a slide might fade in, or slide across the screen.

Transitions should be simple, not distracting.

blocks.heading: Animation makes things move on a slide. ✨
blocks.html:
Example: text can fly in, or a picture can grow bigger.

Use animation only when it helps explain your idea.

blocks.heading: A hyperlink takes you to another place when you click it. 🔗
blocks.html:
It can jump to another slide, a website, or a file.

Hyperlinks are often shown in a different colour.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: How things are placed on a slide 📐
A ready-made design you can use 🎨
How one slide changes to the next 🔄
Movement added to text or pictures ✨
A link you click to jump somewhere else 🔗

question.text: What is a template? 🎨
question.explanation: A template is a ready-made design. It already has colours and a layout for you to use.

question.text: What does a transition do? 🔄
options: Changes one slide to the next
question.explanation: A transition is the way one slide changes into the next slide, like a fade or a slide-across effect.

question.text: What happens when you click a hyperlink? 🔗
options: It jumps you to another place
question.explanation: A hyperlink takes you to another slide, a website, or a file when you click it.

blocks.heading: Bullets list ideas one at a time. •
blocks.html:
Each bullet point is one short idea.

Bullets stop a slide from having too many words.

blocks.heading: Graphics and clip art are pictures on a slide. 🖼️
blocks.html:
They help explain your idea without using words.

Clip art is a simple, ready-made picture.

blocks.heading: Print preview shows what your slides will look like on paper. 🖨️
blocks.html:
Check it before you print.

You can choose to print one slide per page, or many slides per page.

blocks.heading: Document creation means making a new presentation file. 📄
blocks.html:
You give it a name and choose where to save it.

Then you can start adding slides.

blocks.heading: Always proofread your slides before you present. 🔍
blocks.html:
Check for spelling and typing mistakes.

Proofreading means reading your work carefully to find errors.

blocks.title: Put these steps in the order you would do them when making a presentation. 📋

question.text: Why do we use bullet points? •
options: To list short ideas one at a time
question.explanation: Bullet points list short ideas one at a time. This stops a slide from having too many words.

question.text: What does print preview show you? 🖨️
options: What your slides will look like on paper
question.explanation: Print preview shows what your slides will look like before you print them.

question.text: What does proofreading mean? 🔍
options: Reading your work carefully to find errors
question.explanation: Proofreading means reading your work carefully to find spelling and typing mistakes.

blocks.heading: A font is the style of your letters. 🔤
blocks.html:
Some fonts look plain. Some fonts look fancy.

Pick a font that is easy to read.

blocks.heading: Font size is how big or small the letters are. 🔠
blocks.html:
Titles should use a bigger size.

Body text should use a smaller size, but still easy to read.

blocks.heading: Alignment is where text sits on the slide. ↔️
blocks.html:
Text can be left, centre, or right aligned.

Keep your alignment the same on every slide.

blocks.heading: Format means things like bold, italic, or underline. 🔡
blocks.html:
Bold makes text stand out.

Italic can show a special word.

blocks.heading: Spacing is the gap between lines and letters. ↕️
blocks.html:
Too little spacing is hard to read.

Good spacing makes your slide look neat.

blocks.heading: Target audience means the people you are presenting to. 🎯
blocks.html:
Example: young kids, adults, or experts.

You should change your words and pictures to suit them.

blocks.title: Fill in the missing word. Spacing, size, alignment and format are all features of ___.
blocks.explanation: Size, alignment, format and spacing are all features of fonts. They change how your text looks.

question.text: What is font size? 🔠
options: How big or small the letters are
question.explanation: Font size is how big or small the letters are. Titles usually use a bigger size.

question.text: What is a target audience? 🎯
options: The people you are presenting to
question.explanation: Your target audience is the group of people you are presenting to, like young kids or adults.

question.text: Why should you think about your target audience? 🎯
options: So you can add more transitions
So you can choose words and pictures that suit them
So you can make the file bigger
question.explanation: Thinking about your target audience helps you choose the right words and pictures so they understand you.

blocks.heading: The elements of design are the basic building blocks of a slide. 🧱
blocks.html:
They are line, shape, space, and colour.

Every good slide uses these well.

blocks.heading: Line is a mark that can divide or connect parts of a slide. ➖
blocks.html:
A line can separate a title from the body text.

Lines can also guide the eye across the slide.

blocks.heading: Shape means circles, squares, and other forms. 🔵
blocks.html:
Shapes can hold text, or act as buttons.

Shapes can also group similar ideas together.

blocks.heading: Space is the empty area on a slide. ⬜
blocks.html:
Empty space gives your eyes a rest.

Too many words and pictures with no space looks messy.

blocks.heading: Colour can highlight ideas or set a mood. 🌈
blocks.html:
Bright colours can grab attention.

Soft colours can feel calm. Always make text easy to read against the background.

blocks.heading: Balance is the principle of spreading items evenly. ⚖️
blocks.html:
A balanced slide does not feel heavy on one side.

Balance makes a slide feel comfortable to look at.

blocks.heading: Emphasis is the principle of making one thing stand out. ⭐
blocks.html:
You can use size, colour or bold text to add emphasis.

Emphasis helps your audience know what is most important.

blocks.title: Match each design word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: A mark that divides or connects parts of a slide ➖
Circles, squares, and other forms 🔵
The empty area on a slide ⬜
Highlights ideas or sets a mood 🌈
Spreading items evenly on a slide ⚖️
Making one thing stand out ⭐

question.text: What is space in design? ⬜
options: The empty area on a slide
question.explanation: Space is the empty area on a slide. It gives your eyes a rest and stops the slide looking messy.

question.text: What is emphasis? ⭐
question.explanation: Emphasis is making one thing stand out, often using size, colour, or bold text.

question.text: What is balance in design? ⚖️
options: Spreading items evenly on a slide
question.explanation: Balance means spreading items evenly so the slide does not feel heavy on one side.

blocks.heading: Email lets you send messages online. 📧
blocks.html:
It is fast, free, and used at school and work.

Every email has a few key parts.

blocks.heading: To is who gets the email. 👤
blocks.html:
Type the email address of the person here.

Example: sam@school.wa.edu.au

blocks.heading: CC sends a copy to someone else. 📋
blocks.html:
CC means "carbon copy".

Everyone can see who is in the CC list.

blocks.heading: BCC sends a hidden copy. 🙈
blocks.html:
BCC means "blind carbon copy".

People in the To and CC fields cannot see who is in BCC.

blocks.heading: Subject is a short title for your email. 🏷️
blocks.html:
It tells the reader what the email is about.

Example: "Homework due Friday"

blocks.heading: Message and Attachments finish the email. ✉️
blocks.html:
Message — the words you write.
Attachments — files you add, like photos or documents. 📎

blocks.heading: Netiquette is good manners online. 🤝
blocks.html:
It comes from the words "net" and "etiquette".

Be polite, use clear words, and never type in ALL CAPS — it looks like shouting.

blocks.title: Match each email part to its job. 🧩

pairs.definition: The main person who gets the email
Sends a visible copy to someone else
Sends a hidden copy that others cannot see
A short title that says what the email is about
Files added to the email, like photos

question.text: What does BCC do? 🙈
options: Sends a copy that everyone can see
question.explanation: BCC means blind carbon copy. People in To and CC cannot see who is in the BCC list.

question.text: What is netiquette? 🤝
question.explanation: Netiquette means good manners on the internet, like being polite and not shouting in capitals.

question.text: Why should you avoid typing in ALL CAPS? 🔠
options: It looks like you are shouting
It cannot be read by computers
question.explanation: Typing in all capital letters looks like shouting. It is bad netiquette.

blocks.heading: Boolean operators help you search better. 🔍
blocks.html:
They are special words you add to a search.

The three main ones are AND, OR, and NOT.

blocks.heading: AND narrows your search. ✅
blocks.html:
Example: dogs AND training

This finds pages about both dogs and training together.

blocks.heading: OR widens your search. 🔀
blocks.html:
Example: cats OR dogs

This finds pages about cats, or dogs, or both.

blocks.heading: NOT removes a word from your search. 🚫
blocks.html:
Example: jaguar NOT car

This finds pages about the animal, not the car brand.

blocks.heading: A URL is a web address. It has parts. 🌐
blocks.html:
http://www.school.edu.au

http:// = protocol
www = server
school = domain
edu = purpose
au = country

blocks.heading: The purpose part tells you the site type. 🏢
blocks.html:
.com = business 💼
.edu = education 🎓
.gov = government 🏛️
.org = organisation 🤲
.net = network 🔗

blocks.heading: Not every website tells the truth. ⚠️
blocks.html:
Always check a site before you trust it.

Look at who wrote it, and when.

blocks.heading: Check three things to judge a source. 🕵️
blocks.html:
Author — who wrote it?
Publication date — when was it first written?
Date last updated — when was it last checked or fixed?

blocks.title: Match each URL part to what it means. 🧩

pairs.definition: The protocol — the rules for sending the page
The server that holds the website
The purpose — shows it is an education site
The country the website is from

question.text: Which Boolean operator narrows your search to both words? ✅
question.explanation: AND finds pages that include both search words. This makes your search more specific.

question.text: What does .gov tell you about a website? 🏛️
question.explanation: .gov shows the site's purpose is government. It is part of the URL.

question.text: Which is NOT a good way to check if a source is reliable? 🕵️
options: Check when it was last updated
Check if the page has nice colours
question.explanation: Colours do not show if information is true. Check the author and the dates instead.

blocks.heading: Privacy means keeping your information safe. 🔒
blocks.html:
This matters when you use email and social media.

Once you post something, it can be hard to remove.

blocks.heading: Turn off location services when you do not need them. 📍
blocks.html:
Location services share where you are.

Strangers do not need to know where you are.

blocks.heading: Use strong passwords to stay secure. 🔑
blocks.html:
A strong password is long and hard to guess.

Never share your password with anyone.

blocks.heading: Privacy settings control who sees your posts. ⚙️
blocks.html:
Set your social media to "Friends only" where you can.

This stops strangers from seeing your life.

blocks.heading: An avatar is a picture that is not your real face. 🦸
blocks.html:
It can protect your identity online.

Many people use avatars instead of real photos.

blocks.heading: Phishing tricks you into giving away information. 🎣
blocks.html:
A phishing email looks real, but it is fake.

It tries to steal your passwords or bank details.

blocks.heading: There are many other online risks. ⚠️
blocks.html:
Cyberbullying — being mean to someone online. 😢
Identity theft — someone steals your details. 🆔
Cyber stalking — someone watches you online without asking. 👀
SPAM — unwanted junk emails. 🗑️
Viruses — programs that damage your device. 🦠

blocks.title: Spot the phishing email! 🎣
blocks.instruction: Read each email. Decide if it is safe or a phishing scam.

emails.subject: Urgent! Your account will be locked in 24 hours
emails.body: Dear Customer, click this link now and enter your password to stop your account closing today.

redFlags.detail: "bankk-alert.com" is spelled oddly and is not a real bank domain.
Phishing emails often create panic to rush you into clicking.
Real banks never ask for your password by email.

emails.subject: Reminder: Year 11 excursion on Friday
emails.body: Hi students, just a reminder that the excursion is this Friday. Please bring a hat and water bottle.

blocks.title: Match each risk to what it means. 🧩

pairs.definition: A fake email or message that tries to steal your details
Being mean or cruel to someone online
Someone steals your personal details to pretend to be you
Unwanted junk messages sent to many people

question.text: What is phishing? 🎣
options: A fake message trying to steal your details
A privacy setting on social media
question.explanation: Phishing emails look real but are fake. They try to trick you into giving away passwords or bank details.

question.text: Why should you turn off location services? 📍
options: It stops strangers knowing where you are
question.explanation: Location services share where you are. Turning them off keeps strangers from finding you.

question.text: What is identity theft? 🆔
options: Someone steals your personal details to pretend to be you
A virus that slows your computer
question.explanation: Identity theft happens when someone steals your details. They then pretend to be you, often to steal money.

blocks.heading: Copyright protects work that someone created. ©️
blocks.html:
It covers writing, pictures, music, and videos.

You cannot just copy and use it as your own.

blocks.heading: Copyright applies online too. 🌐
blocks.html:
Photos, posts, and videos on social media still belong to their creator.

Sharing them without permission can break copyright rules.

blocks.heading: Citing means giving credit to the creator. ✍️
blocks.html:
If you use someone's words or ideas, say where they came from.

This is called citing a reference.

blocks.heading: A citation usually includes a few details. 📝
blocks.html:
Author — who wrote it.
Title — the name of the work.
Year — when it was published.
Where you found it — website or book.

blocks.heading: A bibliography lists all your sources. 📚
blocks.html:
It goes at the end of your work.

It shows every source you used to find information.

blocks.heading: Always ask before using someone else's work. 🙋
blocks.html:
Some creators allow free use if you give credit.

If you are unsure, ask first or find a free image instead.

blocks.title: Put these steps in order for citing a source correctly. 📚
orderedItems: Find the information you want to use
Note the year it was published
Write the citation next to your work
Add it to your bibliography list

blocks.title: Complete the sentence: A ___ is a list of all the sources you used in your work.
blocks.explanation: A bibliography lists every source you used. It usually goes at the end of your project.

question.text: What does copyright protect? ©️
options: Work that someone created, like writing or photos
question.explanation: Copyright protects creative work such as writing, photos, music, and videos.

question.text: What does citing a source mean? ✍️
options: Giving credit to where you got information
Copying without saying where it came from
question.explanation: Citing means telling people where your information came from. It gives credit to the original creator.

question.text: Where does a bibliography usually go? 📚
options: At the start of your work
At the end of your work
question.explanation: A bibliography is a list of all your sources. It goes at the end of your work.

blocks.heading: Making a digital product needs a plan. 🗂️
blocks.html:
A digital product is something made using technology.

Examples: a website, a poster, a video, an app.

blocks.heading: A digital solution fixes a problem. 💡
blocks.html:
A digital solution uses technology to solve a problem.

Example: an app that reminds you to drink water.

blocks.heading: Who will use your product? That is your target audience. 🎯
blocks.html:
A target audience is the group of people your product is made for.

Example: a game for young children needs simple words and bright colours.

blocks.heading: A design process has 4 stages. 🪜
blocks.html:
1️⃣ Investigate and plan
2️⃣ Design and draft
3️⃣ Produce
4️⃣ Evaluate

You follow these steps in order, every time.

blocks.heading: Stage 1: Investigate and plan. 🔎
blocks.html:
Find out what is needed.

Ask: Who is this for? What is the problem? What will it look like?

blocks.heading: Stage 2: Design and draft. ✏️
blocks.html:
Sketch your ideas on paper or screen.

This is a rough plan, not the final product yet.

blocks.heading: Stage 3: Produce. 🛠️
blocks.html:
Now you make the real product.

You use your draft to build it on the computer.

blocks.heading: Stage 4: Evaluate. ✅
blocks.html:
Check if your product is good.

Ask: Does it work? Does it suit the target audience?

blocks.title: Put the 4 stages of the design process in the correct order. 🪜

question.text: What is a target audience? 🎯
options: The group of people a product is made for
The software used to make a product
question.explanation: The target audience is the group of people who will use your product. You design with them in mind.

question.text: Which stage comes first in the design process? 🪜
question.explanation: You always investigate and plan first. This helps you understand what you need to make.

question.text: What happens in the 'produce' stage? 🛠️
question.explanation: Producing means building the real, finished product using your draft as a guide.

blocks.heading: Time management means using your time wisely. ⏰
blocks.html:
It helps you finish your project on time.

Without it, you might rush at the end.

blocks.heading: A time plan lists tasks and dates. 📅
blocks.html:
It shows what to do and when to do it.

Example: "Week 1 — plan. Week 2 — draft. Week 3 — build."

blocks.heading: A journal records what you did each day. 📓
blocks.html:
Write down your progress and any problems.

This helps you remember what you have finished.

blocks.heading: Use a time plan and a journal together. 🤝
blocks.html:
The time plan shows your goals.

The journal shows what really happened.

blocks.heading: Before you build, you draw your design. ✏️
blocks.html:
This shows others your idea before you start.

It is much faster to fix a drawing than a finished product.

blocks.heading: An annotated diagram is a labelled drawing. 🏷️
blocks.html:
Annotated means it has notes added.

Example: a sketch of a poster with notes like "logo here" and "blue background".

blocks.heading: A storyboard shows steps in order. 🎬
blocks.html:
It is a row of small pictures, like a comic strip.

Each picture shows one screen or one step of your product.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Lists tasks and the dates to do them 📅
Records what you did each day 📓
A drawing with labels and notes 🏷️
A row of pictures showing steps in order 🎬

question.text: What does a time plan show? 📅
options: Tasks and the dates to do them
question.explanation: A time plan lists the tasks you need to do and when you plan to do them.

question.text: What is a storyboard used for? 🎬
options: Showing steps or screens in order
question.explanation: A storyboard is a row of pictures that shows the steps or screens of your product in order.

question.text: Why do we keep a journal during a project? 📓
options: To record what you did and any problems
question.explanation: A journal records your progress each day, including any problems you ran into.

blocks.heading: Evaluating means checking how good something is. 🔍
blocks.html:
You look at your finished product.

You decide what worked well and what did not.

blocks.heading: You can ask 3 different groups for feedback. 🗣️
blocks.html:
1️⃣ Peers — your classmates
2️⃣ Self — you check your own work
3️⃣ Target audience — the people it was made for

blocks.heading: Peer evaluation means classmates give feedback. 🧑‍🤝‍🧑
blocks.html:
A peer is someone at the same level as you.

Peers can spot mistakes you might miss.

blocks.heading: Self evaluation means checking your own work. 🪞
blocks.html:
Ask yourself: Did I meet the brief? Is it easy to use?

Be honest about what could be better.

blocks.heading: Target audience evaluation is the most important. 🎯
blocks.html:
These are the real people who will use your product.

If they like it and can use it, your product is a success.

blocks.heading: Good feedback answers clear questions. ❓
blocks.html:
Example questions: Is it easy to use? Does it look good? Does it solve the problem?

Clear questions give clear answers.

blocks.title: Match each evaluator to who they are. 🧩

pairs.definition: A classmate at the same level as you 🧑‍🤝‍🧑
You, checking your own work 🪞
The real people the product was made for 🎯

blocks.title: Complete this sentence: The ___ is the most important group to ask, because they are the real people who will use the product.
blocks.explanation: The target audience is who the product was made for. Their feedback shows if the product really works for them.

question.text: What does 'evaluate' mean? 🔍
question.explanation: To evaluate means to check how good your finished product is, and what could be improved.

question.text: Who is a 'peer'? 🧑‍🤝‍🧑
options: A classmate at the same level as you
The person who buys the product
question.explanation: A peer is someone at the same level as you, like a classmate. Peers can give useful feedback.

question.text: Why is target audience feedback so important? 🎯
options: They are the real people who will use the product
They always give the kindest feedback
question.explanation: The target audience is who the product is made for. Their feedback shows if it really works for them.

question.text: What does a row do? ➡️
question.explanation: Rows go across the page from left to right. They are numbered 1, 2, 3...

question.text: What is cell B3? 📦
question.explanation: Cell addresses are always column first, then row. So B3 = column B, row 3.

question.text: What is a worksheet? 📄
options: One page in a spreadsheet file
question.explanation: A worksheet is one page in your spreadsheet file. You can have many worksheets in one file.

blocks.heading: The + sign adds numbers together. ➕
blocks.html:
Example: =A1+B1

If A1 = 30 and B1 = 20, the answer is 50.

blocks.heading: The − sign subtracts numbers. ➖
blocks.html:
Example: =A1-B1

If A1 = 30 and B1 = 20, the answer is 10.

blocks.heading: The * sign multiplies numbers. ✖️
blocks.html:
Example: =A1*B1

If A1 = 30 and B1 = 20, the answer is 600.

blocks.heading: The / sign divides numbers. ➗
blocks.html:
Example: =A1/B1

If A1 = 30 and B1 = 5, the answer is 6.

question.text: One of these formulas is broken. Which one is correct for adding A1 and B1? 🧮
question.explanation: Every formula must start with =. So the correct answer is =A1+B1.

question.text: What must every formula start with? 🧮
question.explanation: All formulas must start with the = sign. This tells the spreadsheet to calculate.

question.text: Which formula adds A1 and B1? ➕
question.explanation: The + sign adds numbers. So =A1+B1 adds the values in A1 and B1 together.

question.text: Which symbol divides in a formula? ➗
question.explanation: In spreadsheet formulas, / is used to divide. For example: =A1/B1.

blocks.heading: SUM adds up a group of cells. ➕
blocks.html:
Example: =SUM(B2:B6)

This adds all values from B2 to B6.

blocks.heading: The colon : means from...to. 📏
blocks.html:
B2:B6 means from cell B2 to cell B6.

It includes every cell in between.

blocks.heading: AVERAGE finds the middle value. 📊
blocks.html:
Example: =AVERAGE(B2:B6)

This finds the average of B2 to B6.

blocks.heading: SUM and AVERAGE are called functions. ⚙️
blocks.html:
Functions are shortcuts that do the maths for you.

You do not need to type each cell one by one.

blocks.title: Complete this formula to add up cells B2 to B6: =SUM(___)
blocks.explanation: B2:B6 means from cell B2 to cell B6. The colon : means 'to'.

question.text: What does =SUM(B2:B6) do? ➕
question.explanation: SUM adds all the values in the range together. =SUM(B2:B6) adds B2 + B3 + B4 + B5 + B6.

question.text: What does the colon : mean in a formula? 📏
question.explanation: The colon means 'from...to'. B2:B6 means from B2 to B6, including all cells in between.

question.text: Which function finds the middle value? 📊
question.explanation: AVERAGE adds all the values together. Then it divides by how many there are. That gives the average.

blocks.heading: You can change how a cell looks. 🎨
blocks.html:
This is called formatting.

Formatting makes your data easy to read.

blocks.heading: There are different number formats. 🔢
blocks.html:
Text — shows words and letters.
Number — shows plain numbers.
Currency — adds a $ sign. 💲
Date — shows a date. 📅

blocks.heading: Borders go around the edges of cells. ▦
blocks.html:
They make your table easy to read.

Choose Format → Cells → Border to add them.

blocks.heading: Use colour on your header row. 🌈
blocks.html:
It helps people find the headings fast.

Choose a colour that is easy to read.

blocks.heading: Centre numbers in their cells. ↔️
blocks.html:
Left-align text in their cells.

This follows the standard style used everywhere.

blocks.title: This spreadsheet has 5 formatting problems. Click each numbered spot to find them! 🔍
blocks.instruction: Interactive hotspot activity (see archived Foundations app for the original visual version).

question.text: Which format adds a $ sign? 💲
question.explanation: The Currency format adds a dollar sign and shows two decimal places. For example: $12.50.

question.text: Where should you add a background colour? 🌈
question.explanation: Colouring the header row helps people find the headings quickly. Too much colour makes data hard to read.

question.text: How should you align numbers in a cell? ↔️
question.explanation: Numbers should be centred in their cells. Text is usually left-aligned. This is the standard style.

blocks.heading: A chart turns numbers into a picture. 📊
blocks.html:
Charts make data easy to understand.

You can see patterns much faster with a chart.

blocks.heading: Step 1: Select the data you want to chart. 🖱️
blocks.html:
Click and drag to highlight the cells.

Include the headings so the chart has labels.

blocks.heading: Step 2: Click Insert, then click Chart. 📥
blocks.html:
The Insert menu is at the top of the screen.

This opens the chart builder.

blocks.heading: Step 3: Choose your chart type. 📈
blocks.html:
Column, Bar, Line, or Pie charts all work.

Pick the one that suits your data best.

blocks.heading: Step 4: Give your chart a title. 🏷️
blocks.html:
A good title tells the reader what the chart shows.

Example: "Test Scores for Class 11A"

blocks.heading: Step 5: Move the chart below your data. ⬇️
blocks.html:
Click and drag the chart to move it.

Placing it below keeps your data easy to find.

blocks.title: Put these chart-making steps in the correct order. 📊

question.text: What is the first step to make a chart? 📊
question.explanation: Always select your data first. This tells the spreadsheet what information to use in the chart.

question.text: Where do you click to start a chart? 📥
question.explanation: Click Insert at the top of the screen, then click Chart. This opens the chart builder.

question.text: Where should you place the finished chart? ⬇️
question.explanation: Placing the chart below the data keeps everything together and easy to read.

blocks.heading: A blog is a personal website. 📝
blocks.html:
Someone writes regular posts on a blog.

People read it to follow their ideas and stories.

blocks.heading: A forum is an online notice board. 💬
blocks.html:
People visit forums to discuss topics together.

Anyone can ask a question or give an answer.

blocks.heading: News sites share current news articles. 📰
blocks.html:
You can read the latest news online anytime.

Examples: ABC News, BBC, The Guardian.

blocks.heading: Photo-sharing sites let you post photos online. 📸
blocks.html:
You can share photos with friends and followers.

Instagram and Flickr are popular examples.

blocks.heading: Online games let you play with others. 🎮
blocks.html:
You connect to other players over the internet.

You can play with friends or strangers worldwide.

blocks.heading: YouTube lets you watch and share videos. ▶️
blocks.html:
Anyone can upload a video to YouTube.

Billions of people watch YouTube every day.

blocks.title: Match each online tool to what it does. 🧩

pairs.definition: A personal website with regular written posts
An online notice board for discussing topics
A site where you upload and share photos
A site where you watch and upload videos

question.text: What is a blog? 📝
options: A personal website with regular posts
question.explanation: A blog is a personal website. The writer posts regularly about topics they care about.

question.text: What is a forum used for? 💬
options: Discussing topics with other people online
question.explanation: A forum is like an online notice board. People post questions and replies to discuss topics.

question.text: Which tool is best for watching and sharing videos? ▶️
question.explanation: YouTube is designed for watching and sharing videos. Anyone can upload a video.

blocks.heading: E-learning is learning that happens online. 💻
blocks.html:
You use a device and the internet to learn.

You do not need to be in a classroom.

blocks.heading: There are many e-learning tools. 📱
blocks.html:
Khan Academy — free lessons on many subjects.
YouTube — millions of tutorial videos.
Duolingo — learn a new language for free.

blocks.heading: E-learning has great advantages. ✅
blocks.html:
You can learn from anywhere in the world.
You go at your own pace — fast or slow.
Many resources are completely free.

blocks.heading: E-learning also has some challenges. ⚠️
blocks.html:
You need internet access to learn online.
It is easy to get distracted at home.
You do not have a teacher nearby to help.

blocks.title: Sort each statement into the correct column. 📊

question.text: What does e-learning mean? 💻
question.explanation: E-learning means learning online. You use a device and the internet instead of a classroom.

question.text: Which is an advantage of e-learning? ✅
options: You always have a teacher nearby
You learn at your own pace
You do not need the internet
question.explanation: A big advantage of e-learning is going at your own pace. You can pause, rewind, or repeat.

question.text: Which is a disadvantage of e-learning? ⚠️
question.explanation: E-learning requires internet. If you have no internet, you cannot access the lessons.

blocks.heading: E-commerce is buying and selling things online. 🛒
blocks.html:
You shop using a website or an app.

You pay online and the item arrives at your door.

blocks.heading: E-commerce sites are everywhere. 📦
blocks.html:
eBay — buy and sell second-hand items.
Amazon — millions of products delivered fast.
Kmart online — shop from a major retailer.
Uber Eats — order food to your home.

blocks.heading: Online shopping has great advantages. 🕐
blocks.html:
You can shop from home in your pyjamas.
You can compare prices across many stores.
Shops are open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

blocks.heading: Online shopping also has some risks. ⚠️
blocks.html:
You cannot touch or try items before buying.
There is a risk of scams and fake websites.
You may wait days or weeks for delivery.

blocks.title: Sort each statement into True or False. ✅❌

categories.label: True ✅
items: You can shop online at midnight
eBay is an example of e-commerce
You can compare prices easily online

categories.label: False ❌
items: You can touch items before buying online
E-commerce only works during business hours

question.text: What is e-commerce? 🛒
question.explanation: E-commerce means buying and selling things online. You shop using a website or app.

question.text: Which is an advantage of online shopping? 🕐
options: You can try items before buying
Shops are open 24 hours a day
question.explanation: Online stores never close. You can shop at midnight, on weekends, or on public holidays.

question.text: Which is a risk of online shopping? ⚠️
options: Too many products to choose from
question.explanation: Scam websites look real but steal your money or details. Always check the site is trusted.

blocks.heading: Online banking lets you manage money digitally. 🏦
blocks.html:
You use an app or website to manage your account.

You do not need to visit a bank branch.

blocks.heading: Online banking has many advantages. ✅
blocks.html:
Check your balance anytime, day or night.
Transfer money to someone in seconds.
Pay bills from home without queuing.

blocks.heading: Online banking has some serious risks. 👾
blocks.html:
Hackers may try to steal your login details.
Phishing emails trick you into giving your password.
You could get locked out of your account.

blocks.heading: Stay safe when banking online. 🔒
blocks.html:
Always use a strong, unique password.
Only bank on websites that start with https://.
Log out when you are finished.

blocks.title: Sort each action into Safe or Risky. 🔒⚠️

question.text: What is online banking? 🏦
options: Visiting a bank branch in person
Managing your money through an app or website
question.explanation: Online banking lets you manage your money digitally. You use an app or website instead of visiting a branch.

question.text: Which action keeps you safe in online banking? 🔒
options: Using the same password for all websites
question.explanation: A strong, unique password is harder for hackers to guess. Never reuse passwords across sites.

question.text: What does https:// at the start of a website mean? 🔒
options: The website is free to use
question.explanation: https:// means the website uses encryption. Your data is protected as it travels online.

blocks.heading: Desktop Publishing (DTP) makes layouts with text and images. 🖨️
blocks.html:
You arrange words and pictures on a page.

The result can be printed or shared online.

blocks.heading: DTP software helps you create many things. 📄
blocks.html:
Brochures — folded leaflets about a topic or event.
Calendars — yearly planners with dates and images.
Cards — greetings or invitations for special occasions.
Portfolios — a collection of your best work.

blocks.heading: There are popular DTP programs you can use. 💻
blocks.html:
Microsoft Publisher — comes with Microsoft Office.
Canva — a free online tool for everyone.

blocks.heading: A layout is where you put things on the page. 📐
blocks.html:
You decide where text and images go.

A good layout is easy to read and looks great.

blocks.title: Match each DTP product to its description. 🧩

pairs.definition: A folded leaflet about a topic or event
A yearly planner with dates and images
A greeting or invitation for a special occasion
A collection of your best work

question.text: What does DTP stand for? 🖨️
question.explanation: DTP stands for Desktop Publishing. It is software used to create layouts with text and images.

question.text: Which of these is a DTP program? 💻
question.explanation: Canva is a popular DTP tool. Microsoft Publisher is another example.

question.text: What is a layout? 📐
options: Where you place text and images on a page
question.explanation: A layout is the arrangement of text and images on your page. A good layout is clear and easy to read.

blocks.heading: A template is a ready-made design. 🎨
blocks.html:
You fill it in with your own words and photos.

Templates save you a lot of time.

blocks.heading: Step 1: Open your DTP software. 💻
blocks.html:
Open Microsoft Publisher or Canva.

Make sure you are logged in if needed.

blocks.heading: Step 2: Choose a template. 🎨
blocks.html:
Browse the templates and pick one you like.

Choose a template that suits your project.

blocks.heading: Step 3: Click a text frame and type your words. 📝
blocks.html:
Click on any text area to select it.

Delete the placeholder text and type your own.

blocks.heading: Step 4: Click an image frame and add your photo. 🖼️
blocks.html:
Click on the image area to select it.

Upload your own photo to replace the placeholder.

blocks.heading: Step 5: Save your finished document. 💾
blocks.html:
Save your work so you do not lose it.

You can save as a PDF to share or print.

blocks.title: Put these steps in the correct order for using a template. 🎨
orderedItems: Click a text frame and type your words
Add your photo to the image frame

question.text: What is a template? 🎨
options: A ready-made design you fill in
question.explanation: A template is a ready-made design. You replace the placeholder text and images with your own.

question.text: What should you do after choosing a template? 📝
options: Click a text frame and type your words
question.explanation: After choosing a template, click a text frame and replace the placeholder text with your words.

question.text: What is the last step when using a template? 💾
question.explanation: Always save your work last. You can save as a PDF to share or print your document.

blocks.heading: An image frame holds a photo or picture. 🖼️
blocks.html:
Image frames have a picture icon inside.

Click the frame to add or change the image.

blocks.heading: A text frame holds words. 📝
blocks.html:
Text frames usually have placeholder text inside.

Click the frame and type to add your words.

blocks.heading: Click a frame to select it. 🖱️
blocks.html:
A selected frame shows handles around its edges.

Then you can add or edit the content inside.

blocks.heading: You can move and resize any frame. ↔️
blocks.html:
Drag the frame to move it anywhere on the page.

Drag a corner handle to make it bigger or smaller.

blocks.title: Click each frame on the layout. Find all 4 frames! 🖼️📝
blocks.instruction: Interactive hotspot activity (see archived Foundations app for the original visual version).

question.text: What does an image frame hold? 🖼️
question.explanation: An image frame holds a photo or picture. Click it to add or replace the image.

question.text: How do you select a frame? 🖱️
question.explanation: Click a frame to select it. You will see handles appear around the edges.

question.text: How do you resize a frame? ↔️
question.explanation: Drag a corner handle to resize a frame. Drag from the middle to move it instead.

blocks.heading: Insert adds new content to your page. ➕
blocks.html:
Use Insert to add a new photo, text box, or shape.

Find it in the menu at the top of the screen.

blocks.heading: Rotate turns an object to a different angle. 🔄
blocks.html:
Drag the rotate handle above the object.

You can turn images and text frames sideways.

blocks.heading: Resize makes an object bigger or smaller. ↔️
blocks.html:
Drag a corner handle to change the size.

Hold Shift to keep the shape the same.

blocks.heading: Order moves an object in front of or behind others. 📚
blocks.html:
Right-click and choose Bring to Front or Send to Back.

This fixes objects that hide behind each other.

blocks.heading: A colour scheme picks matching colours for the page. 🎨
blocks.html:
Choose a colour scheme so all colours match.

This makes your document look professional.

blocks.title: Match each problem to the correct editing tool. 🛠️

pairs.term: I want to make my photo bigger
pairs.definition: Resize

pairs.term: I want to turn my image sideways
pairs.definition: Rotate

pairs.term: My text is hiding behind the photo
pairs.definition: Order

pairs.term: I want all my colours to match
pairs.definition: Colour scheme

question.text: Which tool adds a new photo to your page? ➕
question.explanation: Use Insert to add new content. Find it in the menu at the top of the screen.

question.text: Your text is hidden behind a photo. Which tool fixes this? 📚
question.explanation: Order moves objects in front of or behind each other. Use Bring to Front or Send to Back.

question.text: Which tool makes sure all your colours match? 🎨
question.explanation: A colour scheme picks matching colours for your whole page. This makes your work look professional.

blocks.heading: Resolution is how sharp your photo is. 📷
blocks.html:
High resolution means more detail in the photo.

Always use the highest resolution setting you can.

blocks.heading: File format is how your photo is saved. 💾
blocks.html:
JPEG — small file size, great for sharing online.
PNG — larger file, keeps more detail.

JPEG is the most common format for photos.

blocks.heading: Focus makes your subject sharp and clear. 🎯
blocks.html:
Tap the screen to focus on your subject.

If the subject is blurry, the photo will not look good.

blocks.heading: Exposure controls how bright the photo is. ☀️
blocks.html:
Too much light makes a washed-out photo.

Too little light makes a dark, hard-to-see photo.

blocks.heading: Use flash wisely. ⚡
blocks.html:
Turn flash on in dark rooms to light your subject.

Turn flash off in bright light — it can wash things out.

blocks.title: Sort each photo scenario into Good Photo or Bad Photo. 📷

categories.label: Good Photo ✅
items: The subject is in focus and well lit
The photo is sharp with natural light

categories.label: Bad Photo ❌
items: The photo is very dark and blurry
The photo is washed out from too much flash
The subject is tiny and hard to see

question.text: What does resolution mean? 📷
options: How big the photo file is
question.explanation: Resolution means how sharp and detailed a photo is. Higher resolution means more detail.

question.text: When should you use flash? ⚡
options: Every time you take a photo
question.explanation: Use flash in dark rooms to light your subject. Turn it off in bright conditions — it can ruin the photo.

question.text: What is the most common file format for photos? 💾
question.explanation: JPEG is the most common photo format. It has a small file size and is great for sharing online.

blocks.heading: The Rule of Thirds divides the frame into 9 boxes. 📐
blocks.html:
Imagine a 3×3 grid over your photo.

Place your subject on one of the four cross points.

blocks.heading: Leading lines guide the viewer's eye. 🛤️
blocks.html:
Use natural lines like roads, fences, or rivers.

The lines lead the eye toward your subject.

blocks.heading: Framing uses objects to surround your subject. 🏠
blocks.html:
Use a doorway, window, or trees as a frame.

This draws attention to the person or object inside.

blocks.heading: Colour contrast makes your subject pop. 🌈
blocks.html:
Place a bright subject against a darker background.

The contrast helps your subject stand out clearly.

blocks.title: Match each composition rule to its description. 📐

pairs.definition: Place subject on a grid cross point
Use natural lines to guide the viewer's eye
Surround your subject with objects
Put a bright subject on a darker background

question.text: What does the Rule of Thirds suggest? 📐
options: Place your subject on a grid cross point
question.explanation: The Rule of Thirds divides the frame into a 3×3 grid. Place your subject on one of the cross points.

question.text: What are leading lines? 🛤️
options: Lines of text in the photo
Natural lines that guide the eye to the subject
question.explanation: Leading lines are natural lines like roads or rivers. They guide the viewer's eye toward your subject.

question.text: Which technique uses a doorway or window around your subject? 🏠
question.explanation: Framing uses objects like doorways or trees to surround your subject. It draws the eye inward.

blocks.heading: Crop cuts the edges to focus on your subject. ✂️
blocks.html:
Drag the crop handles to remove unwanted areas.

This makes your subject fill more of the frame.

blocks.heading: Brightness makes the photo lighter or darker. ☀️
blocks.html:
Increase brightness if the photo is too dark.

Decrease brightness if the photo is too washed out.

blocks.heading: Contrast makes light and dark areas stand out more. 🌓
blocks.html:
High contrast makes colours look bold and punchy.

Low contrast makes everything look flat and dull.

blocks.heading: Rotate straightens a crooked photo. 🔄
blocks.html:
Use rotate to fix a photo taken at an angle.

Turn it until the horizon looks straight.

blocks.heading: Red eye removal fixes red eyes from flash. 👁️
blocks.html:
Flash can make eyes appear red in photos.

The red eye tool removes this effect automatically.

blocks.title: Match each photo problem to the correct editing tool. 🛠️

pairs.term: Too much background in the shot
pairs.definition: Crop
Brightness
Rotate

pairs.term: Subject has red eyes from flash
pairs.definition: Red eye removal

question.text: Which tool removes unwanted edges from a photo? ✂️
question.explanation: Crop cuts the edges of a photo. Use it to remove distracting backgrounds and focus on your subject.

question.text: Your photo looks flat — light and dark look the same. Which tool helps? 🌓
question.explanation: Contrast makes the light areas lighter and the dark areas darker. This makes the photo look more vivid.

question.text: What causes red eye in a photo? 👁️
options: The brightness setting is too high
The flash reflecting in the eyes
question.explanation: Flash can reflect inside the eye and cause red eye. Use the red eye removal tool to fix it.

blocks.heading: Defamation means sharing a false photo that hurts someone. ⚠️
blocks.html:
This can seriously damage a person's reputation.

Defamation is against the law in Australia.

blocks.heading: Bias means only showing one side of the story. 📊
blocks.html:
Choosing only flattering or unfair photos is bias.

Try to show photos that are fair and honest.

blocks.heading: Always ask permission before sharing someone's photo. 🤝
blocks.html:
Everyone has the right to control their own image.

Ask first — even if it is a friend.

blocks.heading: Think before you post. 🤔
blocks.html:
Once a photo is online, it can spread quickly.

You may not be able to take it back.

blocks.title: Sort each action into the Right Thing or Wrong Thing. ✅❌

categories.label: Right Thing ✅
items: You ask a friend before posting their photo
You choose photos that fairly show both sides
You delete a photo when someone asks you to

categories.label: Wrong Thing ❌
items: You edit a photo to make someone look bad
You share a photo that says something false about someone

question.text: What is defamation? ⚠️
options: Sharing a false photo that hurts someone
Taking a photo without a flash
question.explanation: Defamation means sharing false content that damages someone's reputation. It is against the law.

question.text: What should you do before posting a photo of someone? 🤝
question.explanation: Always ask before sharing someone else's photo. Everyone has the right to control their own image.

question.text: What does photo bias mean? 📊
options: Only showing photos that favour one side
question.explanation: Bias means only choosing photos that show one side unfairly. Always try to be fair and honest.

blocks.heading: A computer system is hardware and software working together. 🖥️
blocks.html:
Hardware is the parts you can touch.

Software is the programs that run on it.

blocks.heading: A desktop computer stays in one place. 🖱️
blocks.html:
It has a separate screen, keyboard, and mouse.

You find them in homes, schools, and offices.

blocks.heading: A mobile device is small and easy to carry. 📱
blocks.html:
Laptops, tablets, and phones are mobile devices.

They run on a battery, so you can use them anywhere.

blocks.heading: A server stores files and shares them with other computers. 🗄️
blocks.html:
Many people can connect to one server.

Servers are usually kept in a locked, cool room.

blocks.heading: Hardware does 4 main jobs. ⚙️
blocks.html:
Input — puts data in (keyboard, mouse). ⌨️
Processing — does the thinking (CPU). 🧠
Output — shows the result (screen, printer). 🖨️
Storage — saves data for later (hard drive). 💾

blocks.heading: Communication hardware connects computers together. 📶
blocks.html:
A Wi-Fi card or network cable lets a computer go online.

This lets it send and receive data.

blocks.heading: Software is split into 3 types. 💿
blocks.html:
Operating system — runs the whole computer (Windows, macOS). 🖥️
Application — software you use for a task (Word, games). 📝
Utility — software that fixes or cleans the computer (antivirus). 🧹

blocks.title: Match each computer type to its description. 🧩

pairs.definition: Stays in one place, has separate parts 🖱️
Small, carried around, runs on battery 📱
Stores files for many other computers 🗄️

question.text: What does a server do? 🗄️
options: Stores and shares files with other computers
question.explanation: A server stores files and shares them with many other computers at the same time.

question.text: Which hardware job does a keyboard do? ⌨️
question.explanation: A keyboard is an input device. It puts data into the computer.

question.text: What type of software is antivirus? 🧹
question.explanation: Antivirus is a utility. Utility software fixes or cleans the computer system.

blocks.heading: Computers need physical care to keep working well. 🛡️
blocks.html:
Looking after the hardware stops damage.

It also makes the computer last longer.

blocks.heading: Ventilation means letting air flow around the computer. 💨
blocks.html:
Computers get hot when they run.

Leave space around the computer so air can move and cool it down.

blocks.heading: Blocked vents cause overheating. 🥵
blocks.html:
Never put a computer against a wall or in a closed box.

Overheating can damage parts inside.

blocks.heading: Keep liquids away from your computer. 🥤
blocks.html:
A spilled drink can ruin the keyboard or the inside parts.

Always keep cups and bottles away from your computer.

blocks.heading: Dust can build up inside a computer. 🌫️
blocks.html:
Dust traps heat and can stop fans from spinning.

Clean dust away regularly with a soft cloth or air duster.

blocks.heading: Dust minimisation keeps a computer running cool. ❄️
blocks.html:
Keep the computer away from carpets and dusty areas.

Wipe down the desk and screen often.

blocks.title: Match each care strategy to why it matters. 🧩

pairs.definition: Lets air flow so the computer does not overheat 💨
Stops spills damaging the hardware 🥤
Stops dust trapping heat and clogging fans 🌫️

question.text: Why does a computer need good ventilation? 💨
question.explanation: Ventilation lets air flow around the computer. This stops it from overheating and getting damaged.

question.text: What can dust do inside a computer? 🌫️
question.explanation: Dust traps heat and can stop fans from spinning properly. This can damage the computer over time.

blocks.title: Fill in the blank: Never place a drink near your keyboard, because a spill could damage the ___.
blocks.explanation: A spilled liquid can get inside the computer and damage the hardware parts.

blocks.heading: External threats can damage your computer or steal your data. 🦠
blocks.html:
A threat is something that can harm your computer system.

Threats can come from the internet or from other devices.

blocks.heading: Antivirus software finds and removes viruses. 🛡️
blocks.html:
A virus is harmful software that can damage files.

Antivirus software scans your computer to find and stop it.

blocks.heading: A firewall blocks unwanted traffic from the internet. 🧱
blocks.html:
It checks data coming in and out of your computer.

A firewall stops dangerous connections before they cause harm.

blocks.heading: WSH stands for Workplace Safety and Health. 🦺
blocks.html:
WSH rules keep you safe while using ICT.

They help to prevent injuries and health problems.

blocks.heading: Use electrical equipment safely. 🔌
blocks.html:
Check cords are not frayed or damaged.

Never overload a power point with too many plugs.

blocks.heading: Watch how much time you spend using technology. ⏱️
blocks.html:
Sitting too long can hurt your eyes, neck, and back.

Take regular breaks away from the screen.

blocks.title: Match each tool or rule to what it does. 🧩

pairs.definition: Finds and removes harmful viruses 🛡️
Blocks unwanted traffic from the internet 🧱
Protects your eyes, neck, and back ⏱️

question.text: What does a firewall do? 🧱
options: Blocks unwanted traffic from the internet
question.explanation: A firewall checks data going in and out of your computer. It blocks dangerous connections.

question.text: What does WSH stand for? 🦺
question.explanation: WSH stands for Workplace Safety and Health. These rules help keep people safe while using ICT.

question.text: Why should you take breaks from screen time? ⏱️
options: To protect your eyes, neck, and back
question.explanation: Sitting and looking at a screen for too long can hurt your eyes, neck, and back. Breaks help prevent this.

blocks.heading: Formatting changes how a document looks. 🎨
blocks.html:
It does not change the words.

Good formatting makes a document easy to read.

blocks.heading: Colour can make text stand out. 🌈
blocks.html:
Use colour on headings, not whole paragraphs.

Too much colour makes a page hard to read.

blocks.heading: Graphics are pictures inside your document. 🖼️
blocks.html:
A graphic could be a photo, a logo, or a chart.

Graphics help explain your ideas.

blocks.heading: Shading adds a background colour to text. 🟦
blocks.html:
Shading is often used behind a heading or a table row.

It helps that part stand out from the rest.

blocks.heading: Borders are lines around text or tables. ▭
blocks.html:
Borders separate one part of a page from another.

They make tables and boxes easy to see.

blocks.heading: Alignment lines up your text. ↔️
blocks.html:
Horizontal alignment — left, centre, right, or justified across the page.

Vertical alignment — top, middle, or bottom of the page or cell.

blocks.heading: A table organises information into rows and columns. 🔢
blocks.html:
Tables are good for lists, prices, or timetables.

You can add borders and shading to a table too.

blocks.title: Match each formatting feature to what it does. 🧩

pairs.definition: Makes text or headings stand out 🌈
Pictures placed inside a document 🖼️
A background colour behind text 🟦
Lines drawn around text or tables ▭
Organises information into rows and columns 🔢

question.text: What does shading do? 🟦
options: Adds a background colour behind text
Draws a line around a table
Lines up text in the centre
question.explanation: Shading adds a background colour. It is often used behind headings or table rows to make them stand out.

question.text: Which alignment spreads text evenly across the whole page width? ↔️
question.explanation: Justified alignment spreads text so it touches both the left and right margins evenly.

question.text: What is a table best used for? 🔢
options: Organising information into rows and columns
Adding a picture to a page
question.explanation: A table organises information into rows and columns. It is good for lists, prices, and timetables.

blocks.heading: Editing means changing your document. ✏️
blocks.html:
You might add words, delete words, or move a sentence.

You edit to make your document better.

blocks.heading: Proofreading means checking for mistakes. 🔍
blocks.html:
You check spelling, grammar, and punctuation.

Always proofread before you print or send a document.

blocks.heading: The thesaurus finds other words with the same meaning. 📖
blocks.html:
It helps you find a better word.

Example: instead of "good", the thesaurus might suggest "great" or "excellent".

blocks.heading: Find and replace can change a word everywhere at once. 🔁
blocks.html:
Type the word to find, and the new word to use instead.

Click "Replace All" to change every match in one go.

blocks.heading: Print preview shows what your page will look like before you print it. 👀
blocks.html:
It helps you spot problems first.

This saves paper and ink.

blocks.heading: Print options let you choose how a document prints. 🖨️
blocks.html:
Number of copies — how many you want.
Page range — all pages, or just some.
Colour or black & white — saves ink if you choose black & white.

blocks.title: Put these steps in order for printing a document. 🖨️
orderedItems: Open print preview to check the layout

question.text: What does the thesaurus tool do? 📖
options: Finds other words with a similar meaning
question.explanation: A thesaurus suggests words with a similar meaning. It helps you find a better word to use.

question.text: You typed "colour" 20 times but want to change it to "shade" everywhere. What is the fastest way? 🔁
options: Delete the whole document and start again
question.explanation: Find and Replace changes every matching word in one go. It is much faster than fixing each one by hand.

question.text: Why should you use print preview? 👀
options: It checks your spelling for you
It lets you see the page before you print it
question.explanation: Print preview shows exactly what will print. It helps you spot layout problems before wasting paper and ink.

blocks.heading: Data is raw facts. Information is data that makes sense. 🧠
blocks.html:
Data — "28, 31, 19" on their own.

Information — "28°C, 31°C, 19°C — this week's temperatures." Now it has meaning.

blocks.heading: A password protects your files and accounts. 🔒
blocks.html:
A strong password is hard for others to guess.

Never share your password with anyone.

blocks.heading: A file is one single document. A folder holds many files. 🗂️
blocks.html:
Folders help you keep similar files together.

Example: a "School" folder full of assignment files.

blocks.heading: A naming convention is a consistent way to name files. 🏷️
blocks.html:
Good naming makes files easy to find later.

Example: Essay_Draft1_2026-06-12.docx tells you what it is and when it was made.

blocks.heading: Version control keeps track of changes to a document over time. 🔄
blocks.html:
Each saved version is called a document version.

This lets you go back to an older version if you make a mistake.

blocks.heading: Good habits keep your digital workspace tidy. ✅
blocks.html:
Use clear folder names.
Save new versions instead of overwriting good work.
Keep your password private and strong.

blocks.title: Match each term to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Raw facts with no meaning on their own 🧠
Data that has been given meaning
Holds many related files together 🗂️
A consistent way to name your files 🏷️
A saved copy of a document at one point in time 🔄

question.text: Which of these is information, not just data? 🧠
options: "15, 22, 9 students absent each day this week"
question.explanation: Information is data that has been given meaning. The numbers alone are just data; explaining what they mean makes it information.

question.text: Which file name follows a good naming convention? 🏷️
question.explanation: A good file name clearly tells you what the file is and when it was made. This makes it easy to find later.

question.text: What is version control used for? 🔄
options: Protecting a file with a password
Keeping track of changes to a document over time
question.explanation: Version control keeps track of saved versions of a document. This lets you see or restore an earlier version if needed.

blocks.heading: A backup is an extra copy of your files. 💾
blocks.html:
If your device breaks or a file is deleted, the backup is safe.

Always keep more than one copy of important work.

blocks.heading: A local backup is stored on a device near you. 🏠
blocks.html:
Examples: a USB drive, an external hard drive, or a second computer.

Risk: if your house has a fire or flood, a local backup can be lost too.

blocks.heading: A remote backup is stored somewhere else, often online. ☁️
blocks.html:
Examples: Google Drive, OneDrive, or iCloud.

Benefit: it is safe even if your own device is lost, stolen, or damaged.

blocks.heading: The best plan uses both local and remote backups. ✅
blocks.html:
Local backups are fast to access.
Remote backups protect you if something happens at home.
Using both gives you the most protection.

blocks.heading: A file format is the type of file you save. 📄
blocks.html:
The format is shown after the dot in a file name.

Example: Essay.docx — the format is .docx

blocks.heading: Each file format is linked to certain software. 🔗
blocks.html:
.docx — opens in word processing software.
.xlsx — opens in spreadsheet software.
.pdf — opens in a PDF reader.
.jpg / .png — opens in image software.

blocks.heading: Your computer uses the file format to choose the right software. 🖥️
blocks.html:
This is called a software association.

That is why double-clicking a .docx file opens your word processor automatically.

blocks.title: Sort each backup example into local or remote. 📊

question.text: What is a remote backup? ☁️
options: A copy stored on a USB drive at home
A copy stored somewhere else, often online
The original file you are working on
question.explanation: A remote backup is stored away from your home, often online. This keeps it safe even if your device is lost or damaged.

question.text: Why is it risky to only use a local backup? 🏠
options: Local backups cost too much money
A fire or flood at home could destroy both copies
Local backups cannot be opened on any device
question.explanation: If your only backup is local, a disaster at home (like fire or flood) could destroy your original file and your backup together.

question.text: What does a software association do? 🔗
options: It links a file format to the software that opens it
It backs up your files automatically
question.explanation: A software association links a file format (like .docx) to the correct software (like a word processor) so it opens automatically.

blocks.heading: A presentation is a set of slides. 🖥️
blocks.html:
In Year 11 you learned to make slides with text and pictures.

In Year 12 you learn how to make slides move and link to other places.

blocks.heading: A transition is the change between two slides. 🔄
blocks.html:
It happens when one slide ends and the next slide starts.

Example: the old slide fades away and the new slide fades in.

blocks.heading: Animation moves things on ONE slide. ✨
blocks.html:
A transition changes between slides.

An animation moves an object, like text or a picture, while you are still on the same slide.

blocks.heading: Use transitions and animations with care. ⚠️
blocks.html:
Too many effects can be distracting.

The audience should focus on your message, not on flashy effects.

blocks.heading: A hyperlink is a clickable link. 🔗
blocks.html:
Click it to jump to another slide, a website, or a file.

Hyperlinks help the audience move around quickly.

blocks.heading: Print preview shows your slides before printing. 🖨️
blocks.html:
It lets you check the layout looks right.

You can choose to print slides, handouts, or notes pages.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: The change between two slides 🔄
Movement of an object on one slide ✨
A clickable link to another place 🔗
Shows slides before you print them 🖨️

question.text: What is a transition? 🔄
options: Movement of text on one slide
question.explanation: A transition is the change you see when moving from one slide to the next slide.

question.text: What does an animation do? ✨
options: Moves an object on one slide
question.explanation: Animation moves an object, like a picture or text box, while you stay on the same slide.

question.text: Why should you use effects with care? ⚠️
options: They can distract the audience from your message
question.explanation: Too many transitions and animations distract the audience. Your message should always come first.

blocks.heading: Elements of design are the basic building blocks of a slide. 🧱
blocks.html:
There are four elements you need to know:

Line, shape, space, and colour.

blocks.heading: A line connects or separates things. ➖
blocks.html:
Lines can guide the eye around the slide.

Example: a line under a heading separates it from the body text.

blocks.heading: A shape is a closed area, like a circle or box. ⬛
blocks.html:
Shapes can hold text or pictures.

Shapes can also group related items together.

blocks.heading: Space is the empty area on a slide. 🌌
blocks.html:
It is also called white space.
