Empty space stops a slide from looking too busy and crowded.

blocks.heading: Colour sets the mood and draws attention. 🎨
blocks.html:
Bright colours grab attention fast.

Calm colours, like blue or grey, feel more relaxed and professional.

blocks.heading: Use colour the same way on every slide. 🌈
blocks.html:
This keeps your presentation looking neat.

Example: always use the same colour for headings.

blocks.title: Match each element of design to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Connects or separates things, and guides the eye ➖
A closed area, like a box or circle ⬛
The empty area on a slide 🌌
Sets the mood and draws attention 🎨

question.text: What is another name for space in design? 🌌
question.explanation: Empty space on a slide is called white space. It stops the slide from looking too crowded.

question.text: What is a shape used for in design? ⬛
options: To guide the eye between two points
To hold text or pictures, or group items
To remove colour from a slide
question.explanation: A shape is a closed area. It can hold text or pictures, or group related items together.

question.text: Which of these is one of the four elements of design? ➖
question.explanation: The four elements of design are line, shape, space, and colour. Balance and unity are principles of design, not elements.

blocks.heading: Principles of design are the RULES for using the elements. 📐
blocks.html:
Elements are the building blocks (line, shape, space, colour).

Principles are the rules for arranging them well: balance, emphasis, and unity.

blocks.heading: Balance means items are spread out evenly. ⚖️
blocks.html:
A balanced slide does not feel heavy on one side.

Example: do not put all your pictures on the left and leave the right empty.

blocks.heading: See the difference between balanced and unbalanced. ⚖️
blocks.html: The left slide is unbalanced. The right slide is balanced.<svg viewBox="0 0 280 130" style="display:block;width:100%;margin-top:14px;border-radius:8px" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect width="280" height="130" fill="#f8fafc" rx="6" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="8" y="8" width="122" height="114" rx="4" fill="white" stroke="#fca5a5" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="35" cy="30" r="12" fill="#fca5a5"/><circle cx="60" cy="30" r="12" fill="#fca5a5"/><circle cx="35" cy="58" r="12" fill="#fca5a5"/><text x="69" y="108" font-family="Arial,sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle">Unbalanced</text><rect x="150" y="8" width="122" height="114" rx="4" fill="white" stroke="#86efac" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="178" cy="30" r="12" fill="#86efac"/><circle cx="244" cy="30" r="12" fill="#86efac"/><circle cx="178" cy="95" r="12" fill="#86efac"/><circle cx="244" cy="95" r="12" fill="#86efac"/><text x="211" y="108" font-family="Arial,sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#16a34a" text-anchor="middle">Balanced</text></svg>

blocks.heading: Emphasis makes one thing stand out. ⭐
blocks.html:
Emphasis has two parts: contrast and proportion.

These both help the most important thing get noticed first.

blocks.heading: Contrast uses different colours to stand out. 🌓
blocks.html:
Example: dark text on a light background.

High contrast is easy to read from across a room.

blocks.heading: Proportion is the SIZE of one thing compared to another. 📏
blocks.html:
This is a new word for Year 12.

Example: make your title BIG and your footnote small. The size difference shows what matters most.

blocks.heading: Unity means everything looks like it belongs together. 🧩
blocks.html:
This is also a new word for Year 12.

A presentation has unity when every slide uses the same fonts, colours, and style.

blocks.heading: Unity vs balance — know the difference. 🔍
blocks.html:
Balance is about ONE slide looking even.

Unity is about ALL slides looking like they belong to the same set.

blocks.title: Match each principle of design to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Items are spread out evenly on a slide ⚖️
Different colours make something stand out 🌓
The size of one thing compared to another 📏
All slides look like they belong together 🧩

blocks.title: Fill in the blank: Making your title bigger than your footnote is an example of ___.
blocks.explanation: Proportion is the size of one thing compared to another. A bigger title shows it is more important than a small footnote.

question.text: What does balance mean in design? ⚖️
options: One slide uses many bright colours
question.explanation: Balance means items on a slide are spread out evenly. The slide should not feel heavy on one side.

question.text: Emphasis is made up of which two things? ⭐
question.explanation: Emphasis has two parts: contrast (different colours) and proportion (different sizes). Both help one thing stand out.

question.text: A presentation where every slide uses the same fonts and colours has good... 🧩
question.explanation: Unity means all slides look like they belong together. Using the same fonts and colours creates unity.

blocks.heading: Big projects need a plan. 🗺️
blocks.html:
Making a digital product takes many steps.

A design process is a set of steps you follow in order.

blocks.heading: There are 4 stages in the design process. 🔢
blocks.html:
1. Investigate and plan
2. Design and draft
3. Produce
4. Evaluate

You do them in this order, one at a time.

blocks.heading: Stage 1: Investigate and plan. 🔍
blocks.html:
Find out who will use your product. This is your target audience.

Ask: what do they need? What do they already have?

blocks.heading: Stage 2: Design and draft. ✏️
blocks.html:
Sketch your ideas before you build anything.

A draft could be a drawing, a storyboard, or a simple plan on paper.

blocks.heading: Stage 3: Produce. 🛠️
blocks.html:
Now you actually make the digital product.

You follow your draft from Stage 2 as you build it.

blocks.heading: Stage 4: Evaluate. ✅
blocks.html:
Check your finished product against your plan.

Ask: does it do what the target audience needs?

blocks.title: Put the 4 design process stages in the correct order. 🔢

question.text: What is a target audience? 🎯
options: The people who will use your product
The software you use to build it
question.explanation: The target audience is the group of people your product is made for. You plan around their needs.

question.text: Which stage comes first in the design process? 🔍
question.explanation: You always investigate and plan first. This helps you understand the task before you design or build anything.

question.text: What happens in the Evaluate stage? ✅
options: You check the product against your plan
question.explanation: Evaluate means checking your finished work. You ask if it meets the plan and the needs of the target audience.

blocks.heading: Big projects are often done in teams. 👥
blocks.html:
One person cannot do everything alone.

Each team member gets a different role, or job, to do.

blocks.heading: There are 4 main roles in a project team. 🧑‍🤝‍🧑
blocks.html:
Project manager
Researcher
Media coordinator
Content writer

Each role has its own job to do.

blocks.heading: The project manager keeps the team on track. 📋
blocks.html:
They watch the timeline and check deadlines.

They make sure every team member knows their job.

blocks.heading: The researcher finds information. 🔎
blocks.html:
They look up facts the team needs.

They check that information is true and correct.

blocks.heading: The media coordinator collects images and sound. 🎬
blocks.html:
They find or make photos, video, and audio.

They check that media files are the right type and size.

blocks.heading: The content writer writes the words. ✍️
blocks.html:
They write text for the product, like captions or pages.

They check the writing is clear and easy to read.

blocks.title: Match each role to what they do. 🧩

pairs.definition: Keeps the team on track and checks deadlines 📋
Finds and checks information 🔎
Collects images, video and sound 🎬
Writes the words for the product ✍️

question.text: Who checks that the team meets its deadlines? 📋
question.explanation: The project manager keeps the team on track. They watch the timeline and check deadlines.

question.text: Which role collects photos and video for the project? 🎬
question.explanation: The media coordinator collects images, video, and sound. They check the files are the right type and size.

question.text: What does the content writer do? ✍️
options: Writes the words for the product
question.explanation: The content writer writes the text used in the product, such as captions or page text.

blocks.heading: Teams need rules to work well together. 🤝
blocks.html:
These rules are called group work protocols.

They help everyone know what to do and how to act.

blocks.heading: Strategy 1: Set group work protocols. 📜
blocks.html:
Agree on rules at the start, like meeting times.

Example rule: everyone listens before speaking.

blocks.heading: Strategy 2: Clearly identify roles. 🏷️
blocks.html:
Everyone should know their own job.

This stops two people doing the same task, or no one doing it.

blocks.heading: Strategy 3: Communicate well. 💬
blocks.html:
Talk often and tell others how your work is going.

Ask for help early if you are stuck.

blocks.heading: Strategy 4: Set clear goals and timelines. ⏰
blocks.html:
A goal is what you want to finish.
A timeline is when each part is due.

Clear goals and timelines help the team stay on track.

blocks.heading: Good teamwork makes better projects. 🌟
blocks.html:
When everyone does their role and talks often, the project goes smoothly.

Poor teamwork can make a project late or messy.

blocks.title: Sort each statement into the correct column. 📊

categories.label: Good teamwork ✅
items: The team has a clear timeline

categories.label: Poor teamwork ⚠️
items: No one knows who does what
Team members never talk to each other
There is no deadline for any task

blocks.title: Complete the sentence: A ___ is when each part of the project is due.
blocks.explanation: A timeline shows when each part of the project must be finished by.

question.text: What are group work protocols? 📜
options: The final mark for the project
Rules the team agrees on for how to work together
A list of software to use
question.explanation: Group work protocols are rules the team sets at the start, like meeting times and how to communicate.

question.text: Why is it important to clearly identify roles? 🏷️
options: So the team can skip the evaluate stage
So everyone knows their own job and nothing is missed
So only one person needs to work
question.explanation: Clear roles stop two people doing the same task, or a task being missed completely.

question.text: What helps a team know when each task is due? ⏰
question.explanation: A timeline sets clear dates for when each part of the project should be finished.

blocks.heading: A website is a set of linked pages on the internet. 🌐
blocks.html:
People visit websites to find information, shop, or be entertained.

Each page is connected to other pages by links.

blocks.heading: Web authoring software is used to build websites. 💻
blocks.html:
Authoring means creating something, like writing or building.

Web authoring software helps you create web pages without starting from nothing.

blocks.heading: A basic text editor lets you type plain code. 📝
blocks.html:
Examples are Notepad and TextEdit.

You type the website code yourself, line by line.

blocks.heading: A source code editor is a smarter text editor. 🖥️
blocks.html:
Examples are Visual Studio Code and Sublime Text.

It colours your code and helps you spot mistakes.

blocks.heading: A WYSIWYG tool shows your page as you build it. 👀
blocks.html:
WYSIWYG means "What You See Is What You Get".

Examples are Wix and WordPress. You drag and drop instead of typing code.

blocks.heading: A word processor can also make simple web pages. 📄
blocks.html:
Examples are Microsoft Word and Google Docs.

You can save a document as a web page, but it has fewer features than other tools.

blocks.title: Match each type of web authoring software to its example. 🧩

pairs.definition: Notepad
Visual Studio Code
Wix
Microsoft Word

question.text: What is a website? 🌐
options: A single picture saved on a computer
A set of linked pages on the internet
question.explanation: A website is a set of linked pages on the internet. Links connect the pages together.

question.text: What does WYSIWYG stand for? 👀
options: What You See Is What You Get
Website You Save In Your Gallery
Web Your Site Will You Grow
question.explanation: WYSIWYG stands for "What You See Is What You Get". The page you build looks the same as the finished website.

question.text: Which tool lets you drag and drop instead of typing code? 🖱️
question.explanation: A WYSIWYG tool like Wix lets you drag and drop page elements. You do not need to type any code.

blocks.heading: A hyperlink takes you to another page or website. 🔗
blocks.html:
Click a hyperlink to jump to a new page.

Hyperlinks are often blue or underlined text, or a button.

blocks.heading: Media elements make a website interesting. 🎬
blocks.html:
Media elements are things like photos, videos, and sound.

They help explain information and keep visitors interested.

blocks.heading: A navigation structure helps visitors move around. 🧭
blocks.html:
Navigation is usually a menu bar near the top of the page.

It lists links to the other main pages, like Home, About, and Contact.

blocks.heading: Web authoring software lets you customise settings. ⚙️
blocks.html:
You can change things like page size, fonts, and colours.

Customising settings helps your website match your design.

blocks.heading: A simple page layout has 4 main parts. 📐
blocks.html: The header is at the top. Navigation links are under it. The content is in the middle. The footer is at the bottom.

blocks.title: Match each website feature to what it does. 🧩

pairs.definition: Takes you to another page or website
A photo, video, or sound on the page
A menu that helps visitors move around
Changing fonts, colours, and page size

question.text: What does a hyperlink do? 🔗
options: Takes you to another page or website
question.explanation: A hyperlink takes you to another page or website when you click it.

question.text: Where is the navigation menu usually placed? 🧭
options: In the footer at the very bottom
Near the top of the page
question.explanation: The navigation menu is usually placed near the top of the page, often under the header.

question.text: Which of these is a media element? 🎬
question.explanation: Media elements include photos, videos, and sound. They make a website more interesting.

blocks.heading: Website layout is where you put things on the page. 📐
blocks.html:
A good layout puts the most important content where people look first.

It should be easy to read and not too crowded.

blocks.heading: Colour scheme means the colours used on your website. 🎨
blocks.html:
Pick 2 or 3 colours that work well together.

Using too many colours can make a page look messy.

blocks.heading: Fonts are the style of your text. 🔤
blocks.html:
Pick fonts that are easy to read on a screen.

Use the same font style for all your headings, and another for body text.

blocks.heading: Location of content means where information sits on the page. 📍
blocks.html:
Put the most important information near the top.

Group similar information together so it is easy to find.

blocks.heading: Plan your navigation before you build the website. 🧭
blocks.html:
Decide which pages you need, like Home, About, and Contact.

Then plan how hyperlinks will connect these pages together.

blocks.heading: Use media elements with a purpose. 🖼️
blocks.html:
Only add photos and videos that help explain your content.

Too many media elements can slow down your website.

blocks.title: Fill in the missing word for each design tip. ✏️
blocks.instruction: Type the missing word in each sentence.

pairs.term: Pick 2 or 3 colours that work well together. This is your colour ___.
pairs.definition: scheme

pairs.term: Choose a ___ that is easy to read on a screen.
pairs.definition: font

pairs.term: Put the most important content where people look first. This is your ___.
pairs.definition: layout

pairs.term: Group similar information together. This is the ___ of content.
pairs.definition: location

question.text: What is a colour scheme? 🎨
options: The fonts used on a page
The colours used on a website
question.explanation: A colour scheme is the set of colours used on your website. Pick 2 or 3 that work well together.

question.text: Why should you only use 2 or 3 colours? 🎨
options: Too many colours can make a page look messy
Computers cannot show more than 3 colours
Colours cost extra money to use
question.explanation: Using too many colours can make a website look messy and hard to read.

question.text: What should you decide before you build the website? 🧭
options: Which pages you need and how they link together
question.explanation: Plan your pages and navigation first. This makes it easier to build and link your website.

blocks.heading: Testing means checking your website works properly. 🔍
blocks.html:
Click every hyperlink to make sure it goes to the right place.

Check that photos and videos load correctly.

blocks.heading: Proofreading means checking your spelling and wording. 📖
blocks.html:
Read all the text on your website carefully.

Fix any spelling mistakes or confusing sentences.

blocks.heading: Check your website on different screens. 📱💻
blocks.html:
A website should look good on a phone, tablet, and computer.

Some text or images might need to move on a smaller screen.

blocks.heading: Publishing puts your website on the internet. 🌍
blocks.html:
Before publishing, your website only you can see it.

After publishing, anyone with the link can visit your website.

blocks.heading: Step 1: Test all hyperlinks and media. 🔗
blocks.html:
Click through every link on every page.

Make sure every photo and video appears correctly.

blocks.heading: Step 2: Proofread all the text. 📖
blocks.html:
Check for spelling and grammar mistakes.

Ask someone else to read it too, if you can.

blocks.heading: Step 3: Publish your website. 🌍
blocks.html:
Use the Publish button in your web authoring software.

Your website is now live on the internet.

blocks.title: Put these steps in the correct order for finishing a website. 🌍
orderedItems: Test all hyperlinks and media elements
Proofread all the text for mistakes
Check the website on different screens

question.text: What does testing a website check? 🔍
question.explanation: Testing checks that hyperlinks, photos, and videos all work properly before you publish.

question.text: What does proofreading check? 📖
question.explanation: Proofreading means checking your spelling and wording for mistakes before you publish.

question.text: What happens when you publish a website? 🌍
options: It is deleted from your computer
It goes live on the internet for others to see
It is saved only on your own device
question.explanation: Publishing puts your website on the internet. Anyone with the link can then visit it.

blocks.heading: Social media lets people connect online. 📱
blocks.html:
Social media is websites and apps for sharing and talking.

People use it to post, chat, and stay in touch.

blocks.heading: There are different types of social media. 🗂️
blocks.html:
Facebook — share posts and photos with friends.
Instagram — share photos and short videos.
TikTok — share short videos.
Snapchat — send photos and messages that disappear.

blocks.heading: Social media tools share common features. ⚙️
blocks.html:
A profile — your name, photo, and information.
A news feed — posts from people you follow.
Comments and likes — ways to react to posts.
Messaging — sending private messages to others.

blocks.heading: Connectivity means staying linked to others. 🔗
blocks.html:
Social media connects you to friends and family.

You can talk to people anywhere in the world.

blocks.title: Match each social media feature to what it does. 🧩

pairs.definition: Your name, photo, and information on the app
A list of posts from people you follow
Ways to react to someone's post
Sending a private message to one person

question.text: What is social media? 📱
options: Websites and apps for sharing and talking
question.explanation: Social media is websites and apps. People use them to post, chat, and stay connected.

question.text: What does your profile show on social media? 👤
options: A list of every website you visit
question.explanation: Your profile shows your name, photo, and basic information about you.

question.text: What does connectivity mean on social media? 🔗
options: How fast your internet loads a page
How many photos you have posted
question.explanation: Connectivity means staying linked to others. Social media connects you to people anywhere.

lessons.title: Pros and Cons of Online Collaboration

blocks.heading: Online collaboration means working together online. 🤝
blocks.html:
You can chat, share files, and plan with others.

You do not need to be in the same room.

blocks.heading: Online collaboration has good points. ✅
blocks.html:
Better connectivity — you stay in touch with people far away.
More engagement — more people can join in and share ideas.
Quick to share files, photos, and updates.

blocks.heading: Online collaboration also has risks. ⚠️
blocks.html:
You may share your personal information by accident.
Apps can show your physical location to others.
Strangers may see things you only meant for friends.

blocks.heading: Be careful what you share online. 📍
blocks.html:
Sharing your location can tell strangers where you are.

Sharing personal details can put your safety at risk.

blocks.title: Sort each statement into Advantage or Disadvantage. 📊

categories.label: Advantage ✅
items: Stay connected with people far away
More people can join in and share ideas

categories.label: Disadvantage ⚠️
items: Your location may be shared with strangers
Personal information can leak by accident

question.text: What is online collaboration? 🤝
question.explanation: Online collaboration means working together using the internet, even if you are far apart.

question.text: Which is a disadvantage of online collaboration? ⚠️
options: You can chat with people far away
Your personal information or location may be shared
question.explanation: A risk of online collaboration is accidentally sharing personal information or your location.

question.text: What does increased engagement mean? 📈
options: More people join in and share ideas
question.explanation: Increased engagement means more people are joining in, posting, and sharing ideas together.

blocks.heading: Ethical behaviour means doing the right thing. ✅
blocks.html:
This is true online too, not just in person.

Ethical online behaviour means being kind, honest, and respectful.

blocks.heading: Appropriate online behaviour looks like this. 👍
blocks.html:
Being polite and respectful in comments and messages.
Asking before sharing photos of other people.
Thinking before you post — is it true and is it kind?

blocks.heading: Inappropriate online behaviour looks like this. 👎
blocks.html:
Posting mean comments or bullying someone.
Sharing private photos without asking.
Spreading rumours or lies about someone.

blocks.heading: Your digital reputation is what people think of you online. 🏷️
blocks.html:
Everything you post builds your digital reputation.

Bad posts can follow you for years, even if you delete them.

blocks.heading: Inappropriate behaviour has real consequences. ⚠️
blocks.html:
Digital reputation — others may see you as unkind or untrustworthy.
Relationships — friends and family may lose trust in you.
Employment — employers often check your social media before hiring.

blocks.title: Sort each action as Appropriate or Inappropriate. 🧩

categories.label: Appropriate 👍
items: Asking before sharing a friend's photo

question.text: What is a digital reputation? 🏷️
options: What people think of you based on what you post online
The number of apps you have installed
question.explanation: Your digital reputation is what people think of you based on what you post and do online.

question.text: Why might inappropriate posts affect your employment? 💼
options: Employers often check social media before hiring someone
question.explanation: Many employers check a person's social media before offering them a job.

question.text: Which of these is appropriate online behaviour? 👍
options: Sharing a friend's photo without asking
question.explanation: Thinking before you post helps you avoid hurting others or damaging your reputation.

blocks.heading: Privacy means keeping your information safe. 🔒
blocks.html:
Privacy is your right to control who sees your information.

Online, this includes your photos, messages, and location.

blocks.heading: Protect your privacy online. 🛡️
blocks.html:
Use privacy settings to control who sees your posts.
Do not share your address or phone number publicly.
Think twice before posting your location.

blocks.heading: A code of conduct sets the rules for behaviour. 📋
blocks.html:
A code of conduct is a list of rules for how to behave.

Schools and workplaces use them to keep everyone safe and respectful.

blocks.heading: Why do schools and workplaces need an ICT code of conduct? 🏫
blocks.html:
It tells people how to use computers and the internet properly.
It protects everyone's privacy and safety.
It explains what happens if someone breaks the rules.

blocks.title: Spot the phishing email. 📧
blocks.instruction: Read each email and decide if it is safe or a scam trying to steal your privacy.

emails.subject: Your account will be locked! Verify now
emails.body: We noticed unusual activity. Click this link and enter your password within 24 hours or your account will be deleted.

redFlags.detail: Says your account will be deleted to scare you
Real companies never ask for your password by email

emails.subject: Reminder: ICT code of conduct training next week
emails.body: Hi students, just a reminder that our ICT code of conduct session is on Tuesday in the library. No action needed.

emails.subject: You won a $500 gift card! Claim your prize
emails.body: Congratulations! Enter your full name, address, and phone number here to claim your free gift card before it expires.

redFlags.detail: Random prizes you did not enter are a common scam
Wants your address and phone number
freegiftcardz.net is not a trusted company

blocks.title: Put these steps for protecting your privacy online in order. 🔢
orderedItems: Choose who can see your posts
Avoid sharing your address or phone number

question.text: What does privacy mean online? 🔒
options: Controlling who can see your information
Deleting all your social media accounts
question.explanation: Privacy means you control who can see your information, like photos and location.

question.text: What is a code of conduct? 📋
options: A list of rules for how to behave
question.explanation: A code of conduct is a list of rules. Schools and workplaces use it to guide behaviour.

question.text: Why do schools have an ICT code of conduct? 🏫
options: To keep everyone safe and using technology properly
To stop students from using computers
question.explanation: An ICT code of conduct keeps people safe and explains how to use technology properly.

blocks.heading: Let's remember spreadsheet basics. 📊
blocks.html:
You learned this last year. Let's go over it fast.

A spreadsheet stores data in a grid of rows and columns.

blocks.heading: Rows go across. Columns go up and down. ⬛
blocks.html:
Rows are numbered: 1, 2, 3...
Columns are labelled: A, B, C...

blocks.heading: A cell is one box. A worksheet is one page. 📦
blocks.html:
Cell A2 is in column A, row 2.

A worksheet is one page in a spreadsheet file. You can have many worksheets.

blocks.heading: Formulas calculate. Functions are shortcuts. 🧮
blocks.html:
A formula always starts with =.

Example: =A1+B1 adds two cells.

SUM and AVERAGE are functions. They do the maths for you.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Goes across the page ➡️
Goes up and down the page ⬇️
One single box in the grid 📦
A shortcut that does maths for you ⚙️

question.text: What must every formula start with? 🧮
question.explanation: Every formula starts with the = sign. This tells the spreadsheet to calculate.

question.text: Which function adds up a group of cells? ➕
question.explanation: SUM adds up all the values in a range of cells, like =SUM(B2:B6).

question.text: What is cell B3? 📦
question.explanation: Cell addresses are column first, then row. So B3 = column B, row 3.

blocks.heading: Percentage shows how big a part is. 💯
blocks.html:
A percentage compares a part to a whole.

It is shown with a % sign. Example: 50%.

blocks.heading: You can turn a number into a percentage. 🔢
blocks.html:
Divide the part by the whole. Then multiply by 100.

Example: 20 out of 40 is 50%.

blocks.heading: A percentage formula uses division. ➗
blocks.html:
Example: =B2/C2

If B2 = 20 and C2 = 40, the answer is 0.5.

blocks.heading: Set the cell format to Percentage. 🏷️
blocks.html:
The spreadsheet then shows your answer as a % automatically.

0.5 becomes 50% on the screen.

blocks.heading: Percentages help you compare fairly. ⚖️
blocks.html:
They show a share out of 100, no matter how big the totals are.

This makes results easy to compare.

blocks.title: Complete this formula to find what percentage B2 is of C2: =___
blocks.explanation: Divide the part (B2) by the whole (C2). Then format the cell as a percentage.

question.text: What does a percentage show? 💯
question.explanation: A percentage shows a part compared to a whole, out of 100. It uses the % sign.

question.text: Which formula works out a percentage? 🧮
question.explanation: Divide the part by the whole to get a percentage. Example: =B2/C2.

question.text: 20 out of 40 equals what percentage? 💯
question.explanation: 20 divided by 40 is 0.5. As a percentage, that is 50%.

blocks.heading: Different charts show data in different ways. 📊
blocks.html:
You learned about charts last year.

Now let's learn which chart to pick for the job.

blocks.heading: A column chart compares amounts. 📊
blocks.html:
It uses tall bars standing up.

Good for comparing groups, like sales by month.

blocks.heading: A bar chart compares amounts too. 📊
blocks.html:
It uses bars lying on their side.

Good for comparing items with long names.

blocks.heading: A pie chart shows parts of a whole. 🥧
blocks.html:
Each slice is a percentage of the total.

All slices together always make 100%.

blocks.heading: A line chart shows change over time. 📈
blocks.html:
It uses dots joined by a line.

Good for showing trends, like temperature over a week.

blocks.heading: Pick the chart that fits your data. ✅
blocks.html:
Comparing groups? Use column or bar.
Showing parts of 100%? Use pie.
Showing change over time? Use line.

blocks.title: Match each chart type to what it is best for. 🧩

pairs.definition: Comparing amounts with upright bars 📊
Comparing amounts with sideways bars 📊
Showing parts of a whole 🥧
Showing change over time 📈

question.text: Which chart is best to show parts of a whole? 🥧
question.explanation: A pie chart shows percentages of a whole. All the slices together make 100%.

question.text: Which chart is best to show change over time? 📈
question.explanation: A line chart uses dots joined by a line. This makes it easy to see a trend over time.

question.text: What is the difference between a column chart and a bar chart? 📊
options: Column bars stand up, bar chart bars lie sideways
A bar chart can only show one value
question.explanation: Column charts use upright bars. Bar charts use sideways bars. Both compare amounts.

blocks.heading: Spreadsheets need good design too. 🎨
blocks.html:
Good design helps your audience understand the data.

Your audience is the people who will read it.

blocks.heading: Use colour with a clear purpose. 🌈
blocks.html:
Use colour to highlight important cells, like the header row.

Too many colours make a spreadsheet confusing.

blocks.heading: Readability means easy to read. 👀
blocks.html:
Use a clear font and a sensible size.

Leave space between columns. Don't cram in too much.

blocks.heading: Charts must also be easy to read. 📊
blocks.html:
Always add a title to your chart.

Label each axis so people know what the numbers mean.

blocks.heading: Pick design choices for your audience. 🎯
blocks.html:
A spreadsheet for young children might use bright colours and pictures.

A spreadsheet for a business report should look neat and simple.

blocks.title: Put these design steps in the best order. 🎨
orderedItems: Choose a clear font and size
Add colour to the header row only
Add a title and axis labels to the chart

question.text: Why should you think about your audience first? 🎯
options: It changes how you should design the spreadsheet
It does not matter for spreadsheets
question.explanation: Your audience affects your design choices, like colour, font size, and layout.

question.text: What should every chart have to help readers understand it? 📊
question.explanation: A title tells readers what the chart shows. Labelled axes explain what the numbers mean.

question.text: How much colour should you use in a spreadsheet? 🌈
options: Only where it helps, like the header row
question.explanation: Use colour with a clear purpose, such as highlighting headers. Too much colour makes data hard to read.

blocks.heading: Sound editing software lets you make and change audio. 🎧
blocks.html:
Audio means sound — like music, voice, or sound effects.

Sound editing software is a computer app for working with audio.

blocks.heading: Create means making a brand new sound. 🎹
blocks.html:
Some apps let you make new sounds from nothing.

Example: adding a drum beat or a music track.

blocks.heading: Capture means recording a sound. 🎤
blocks.html:
Capture uses a microphone to record real sound.

Example: recording your voice for a podcast.

blocks.heading: Edit means changing a sound file. ✂️
blocks.html:
Editing can cut out parts you don't want.

Editing can also join two sounds together.

blocks.heading: Effects change how a sound feels. 🌀
blocks.html:
Effects can make a voice echo, sound louder, or sound smoother.

Example: fade in slowly raises the volume from silent to loud.

blocks.title: Match each word to what it means. 🎧

pairs.definition: Making a brand new sound
Recording sound with a microphone
Cutting or joining sound files
Changing how a sound feels, like echo

question.text: What does capture mean in sound editing? 🎤
options: Cutting out part of a song
Recording a sound with a microphone
question.explanation: Capture means recording a real sound using a microphone, like recording your voice.

question.text: Which word describes adding an echo to a voice recording? 🌀
question.explanation: Effects change how a sound feels. An echo is an example of an effect.

question.text: What is sound editing software used for? 🎧
question.explanation: Sound editing software is an app for making and changing audio, like music or voice recordings.

blocks.heading: An audio file format is how a sound is saved. 💾
blocks.html:
Different formats save sound in different ways.

Each format has its own pros and cons.

blocks.heading: WAV is a high-quality audio format. 🎼
blocks.html:
WAV stands for Waveform Audio File Format.

It sounds great but the file size is very large.

blocks.heading: MP3 is a small, popular audio format. 📱
blocks.html:
MP3 squashes the file to make it much smaller.

This makes MP3 great for sharing and storing music.

blocks.heading: WMA was made by Microsoft. 🪟
blocks.html:
WMA stands for Windows Media Audio.

It is small in size, like MP3, but works best on Windows devices.

blocks.heading: Choose your format based on what you need. ⚖️
blocks.html:
Need the best quality for editing? Use WAV.

Need a small file to share online? Use MP3.

blocks.title: Match each audio format to what it is best used for. 🎵

pairs.definition: Best sound quality for editing, but a big file
Small file, great for sharing music online
Small file made by Microsoft, works well on Windows

question.text: Which audio format has the best quality but the largest file size? 🎼
question.explanation: WAV has the best sound quality, but this means the file size is very large.

question.text: You want to share a song online and keep the file small. Which format should you use? 📱
question.explanation: MP3 makes the file much smaller, which is great for sharing and storing online.

question.text: What does WMA stand for? 🪟
question.explanation: WMA stands for Windows Media Audio. It was made by Microsoft.

blocks.heading: A waveform shows what sound looks like. 〜
blocks.html:
Loud parts of the sound look tall on the waveform.

Quiet parts of the sound look flat and small.

blocks.heading: Trim cuts off the start or end of a sound. ✂️
blocks.html:
Use trim to remove silence or mistakes.

Trim keeps the part of the recording you want.

blocks.heading: Fade in and fade out make sound smooth. 📈
blocks.html:
Fade in slowly raises the volume from silent to loud.

Fade out slowly lowers the volume from loud to silent.

blocks.heading: Combining means joining two or more audio files. 🔗
blocks.html:
You can place one sound file after another.

You can also layer sounds, like music under a voice.

blocks.heading: Always save your finished file with a clear name. 📁
blocks.html:
Use a name that says what the file is, like "interview_final.mp3".

This helps you find it again later.

blocks.title: Put these steps for editing a podcast recording in the correct order. 🎙️
orderedItems: Capture the voice recording with a microphone
Trim the silence from the start and end
Add a fade in and fade out
Combine the voice track with background music
Save the finished file with a clear name

question.text: What does trim do to an audio file? ✂️
options: Cuts off the start or end of a sound
question.explanation: Trim removes unwanted parts from the start or end of a recording, like silence or mistakes.

question.text: What happens during a fade out? 📉
options: The volume slowly goes from loud to silent
The volume slowly goes from silent to loud
question.explanation: Fade out slowly lowers the volume until the sound becomes silent.

question.text: What does combining audio files mean? 🔗
options: Joining or layering two or more sound files
Saving a file in WAV format
question.explanation: Combining means joining files one after another, or layering them, like music under a voice.

lessons.title: What Video Editing Software Can Do

blocks.heading: Video editing software lets you turn raw clips into a finished video. 🎬
blocks.html:
You import your video clips, photos, and audio.

Then you arrange and change them to tell your story.

blocks.heading: Multimedia means using more than one type of media together. 🎞️
blocks.html:
Video editors combine videos, images, and audio in one project.

Mixing these makes your video more interesting.

blocks.heading: You can add text on top of your video. 🔤
blocks.html:
Titles — show the name of your video at the start.
Captions — show subtitles or extra words during the video.
Credits — show who made the video at the end.

blocks.heading: Split and clip let you cut a video into pieces. ✂️
blocks.html:
Split cuts one clip into two clips.
Clip (trim) removes the parts you don't want.

This helps you keep only the best parts.

blocks.heading: Transitions, effects, and animations make your video flow. ✨
blocks.html:
A transition is how one clip changes into the next, like a fade.
Effects change how a clip looks, like black and white.
Animations make text or images move on screen.

blocks.heading: Scenes, layout, and timing organise your whole video. 🗂️
blocks.html:
A scene is one part of your story.
The layout is how clips, text, and audio are arranged on the timeline.
Timing is how long each part shows for.

blocks.title: Match each feature to what it does. 🎬

pairs.definition: Text shown at the start with the video's name
Text shown during the video, like subtitles
Cuts one clip into two clips
How one clip changes into the next clip
Makes text or images move on screen

question.text: What does multimedia mean in video editing? 🎞️
options: Combining video, images, and audio together
question.explanation: Multimedia means combining different types of media — video, images, and audio — in one project.

question.text: You want to cut one long clip into two shorter clips. What tool do you use? ✂️
question.explanation: Split cuts one clip into two separate clips. You can then edit or remove each part.

question.text: What is a transition? ✨
options: Text shown at the end of a video
How one clip changes into the next clip
The length of the whole video
question.explanation: A transition is how one clip changes into the next, like a fade or a wipe.

blocks.heading: A timeline shows your whole video in order. ⏱️
blocks.html:
Clips, text, and audio sit on tracks along the timeline.

You drag clips left or right to change their order.

blocks.heading: Trim removes the start or end of a clip. ✂️
blocks.html:
Drag the edge of a clip to make it shorter.

This cuts out boring or shaky footage.

blocks.heading: Layout is how everything is arranged on screen. 🗂️
blocks.html:
Layout includes where text sits, how big a clip is, and what is on top.

A clear layout helps your audience understand your video.

blocks.heading: Timing controls how long each part shows. ⏳
blocks.html:
Show text long enough for people to read it.

Cut clips before they get boring.

blocks.heading: Add audio to support your video. 🔊
blocks.html:
Music sets the mood.
Voiceover explains what is happening.
Sound effects add extra detail.

blocks.title: Put these video editing steps in the correct order. 🎬
orderedItems: Import your video clips, photos, and audio
Split and trim clips to remove unwanted parts

blocks.title: Complete this sentence: trimming a clip makes it ___.
blocks.explanation: Trim removes the start or end of a clip, making it shorter. It helps you cut out parts you don't want.

question.text: What does the timeline show? ⏱️
options: Your whole video laid out in order
question.explanation: The timeline shows all your clips, text, and audio arranged in the order they will play.

question.text: Why might you add a voiceover to your video? 🔊
options: To make the video file smaller
To explain what is happening on screen
question.explanation: A voiceover is spoken narration that explains what is happening in the video.

lessons.title: Video Files, Aspect Ratio and Resolution

blocks.heading: Project files and exported files are different. 💾
blocks.html:
A project file only opens in your editing software. It keeps all your clips and edits so you can keep changing them.

An exported file is the finished video, like an MP4. Anyone can watch it without editing software.

blocks.heading: Always keep your project file. 📁
blocks.html:
If you only save the exported video, you cannot go back and change it.

Keep the project file so you can fix mistakes later.

blocks.heading: Aspect ratio is the shape of your video. 📐
blocks.html:
16:9 is wide — used for YouTube and TVs.
9:16 is tall — used for phone apps like stories and reels.

Pick the shape that matches where your video will be watched.

blocks.heading: Resolution is how sharp your video looks. 🔍
blocks.html:
Higher resolution, like 1080p or 4K, shows more detail.

Lower resolution looks blurry on a big screen.

blocks.heading: Match your settings to your audience. 🎯
blocks.html:
A video for a phone app should be tall (9:16).

A video for a TV or YouTube should be wide (16:9).

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 💾

pairs.definition: An editable file that only opens in the editing software
The finished video that anyone can watch
The shape of the video, like wide or tall
How sharp and detailed the video looks

question.text: Why should you keep your project file? 📁
options: It lets you keep editing the video later
It has a smaller file size
question.explanation: A project file keeps all your clips and edits, so you can open it later and keep changing your video.

question.text: You are making a video for a phone story. Which aspect ratio should you use? 📐
question.explanation: Phone stories and reels are watched on a tall screen, so 9:16 fits best.

question.text: What happens if you use low resolution on a big screen? 🔍
question.explanation: Low resolution has less detail, so the video looks blurry when shown on a big screen.

blocks.heading: Always plan before you film. 📝
blocks.html:
Good planning saves time and stops mistakes.

The design process helps you plan step by step.

blocks.heading: The design process has clear steps. 🔄
blocks.html:
Investigate — who is your audience and what do they need?
Devise — plan your scenes and shots.
Produce — film and edit your video.
Evaluate — check if it works for your audience.

blocks.heading: Know your target audience. 🎯
blocks.html:
Your target audience is the people who will watch your video.

Think about their age and what they like before you plan your video.

blocks.heading: Elements and principles of design still apply to video. 🎨
blocks.html:
Use colour, text, and layout that suit your audience.

Keep your video balanced and easy to follow.

blocks.heading: Project management keeps your video on track. 📅
blocks.html:
Set a timeline for filming and editing.
List what equipment and people you need.

Check your progress so you finish on time.

blocks.title: Put these planning steps in the correct order. 🔄
orderedItems: Investigate your target audience and purpose
Devise a plan for your scenes and shots
Produce the video by filming and editing
Evaluate the finished video with your audience

blocks.title: Match each planning idea to its meaning. 📅

pairs.definition: The people who will watch your video
The steps used to plan and create a video
Planning time and resources to finish on time

question.text: What is a target audience? 🎯
options: The software used to edit the video
The people who will watch your video
The length of the finished video
question.explanation: A target audience is the group of people you are making the video for. Plan your video to suit them.

question.text: Which step of the design process happens first? 🔄
question.explanation: Investigate comes first. You need to understand your audience and purpose before you plan and film.

question.text: Why use project management when making a video? 📅
options: To make the video file smaller
To plan time, people, and equipment so you finish on time
To change the aspect ratio automatically
question.explanation: Project management helps you plan your time, equipment, and people so your video gets finished on time.
